If generative AI is a powerful new tool, then prompt engineering is the discipline of using it effectively. The difference between a generic, unhelpful response and a precisely tailored, brilliant one often comes down to how you structure your request.

Introduction to Prompt Engineering

Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing questions and instructions that elicit the best possible responses from AI systems. It encompasses diverse techniques: the structure of initial questions, strategic use of context, role-playing scenarios, chain-of-thought reasoning, example-based learning, and many others. Each technique serves a different purpose, and understanding when and how to apply them dramatically improves results.

A good introductory book guides you from simple, straightforward prompts through increasingly sophisticated techniques. You'll learn not just the "what" but the "why"—understanding the principles behind effective prompting helps you apply them creatively to novel situations. The best books in this category emphasize real examples you can immediately experiment with and adapt to your own work.

Search Amazon.co.jp for "Prompt Engineering Fundamentals" to explore comprehensive guides.

Official Certification Textbooks

Several organizations have published official textbooks for prompt engineering certification programs. These provide authoritative, standardized knowledge bases that the industry recognizes. By studying from an official curriculum, you're learning what professionals are expected to know and what formal evaluators consider essential competency.

These textbooks synthesize research and practical experience into clearly structured content. Rather than overwhelm you with tangential information, they focus on what matters most. If you're considering prompt engineering as a career track or simply want a comprehensive, trustworthy overview, certification textbooks offer excellent structured learning paths.

Search Amazon.co.jp for "Generative AI Prompt Engineer Certification" to find official study materials.

LLM Development & Engineering

For developers and technical practitioners, understanding how large language models work at a deeper level opens possibilities for custom implementations, fine-tuning, and optimization.

Learning the Theory Behind Large Language Models

While you can use Claude or ChatGPT without ever understanding neural networks, deeper technical knowledge reveals both capabilities and limitations that aren't obvious from surface-level usage. You'll understand why models sometimes "hallucinate," how they handle context, why some tasks are easy while others are hard, and how to structure problems to play to their strengths.

"An Introduction to Large Language Models" by Yuki Yamada (published by Gihyo Shuppan) approaches the subject from a conceptual rather than purely mathematical angle. Starting from Transformer architecture fundamentals and progressing to actual model development, this book is accessible to engineers while remaining rigorous. Understanding how language models process tokens, generate predictions, and learn from training data directly improves your ability to prompt effectively and potentially fine-tune models for specialized applications.

Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)

Sebastian Raschka's "Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)" teaches through doing. Rather than reading about LLM architecture, you build one in Python. This hands-on approach, spanning from the basic mechanics of tokenization through complete model training, creates deep intuitive understanding that abstract explanations cannot match.

This book appeals especially to developers interested in implementing custom models, fine-tuning existing open-source models, or simply gaining profound literacy in how LLMs work internally. The experience of building even a simplified LLM from scratch transforms how you think about these systems. Search Amazon.co.jp for "Build a Large Language Model From Scratch Raschka" to find available editions.

LLM Engineering Handbook

Deploying language models in production environments introduces practical challenges that courses and introductory books rarely address. How do you optimize cost when API calls are expensive? How do you handle errors gracefully? How do you structure applications to use tokens efficiently? The "LLM Engineering Handbook" collects best practices, architectural patterns, and implementation strategies for these real-world concerns.

This resource is invaluable if you're building systems that will serve actual users, handle production loads, and need to be maintained long-term. Topics include prompt caching strategies, token budget optimization, error recovery patterns, API integration architecture, and performance tuning. Whether you're building an internal tool or customer-facing product powered by LLMs, this handbook accelerates your path from prototype to robust production system.

Search Amazon.co.jp for "LLM Engineering Handbook" for the current edition.

AI Agents & Multi-Agent Systems

The next frontier in AI development moves beyond single models responding to queries toward systems where multiple AI agents work together, specializing in different tasks and coordinating to solve complex problems.

Designing Multi-Agent Systems

Victor Dibia's "Designing Multi-Agent Systems" explores how to architect systems where multiple AI agents collaborate. Rather than one large model handling everything, you distribute tasks among specialized agents that communicate, share information, and coordinate actions. This approach proves particularly powerful for complex reasoning, multi-step workflows, and scenarios requiring diverse expertise.

Multi-agent systems amplify the capabilities of models like Claude. By orchestrating multiple instances with different roles and contexts, you can achieve results impossible for a single agent. One agent might gather information, another synthesizes and analyzes it, a third generates options, and a fourth evaluates them. Understanding the design patterns, communication protocols, and failure modes of such systems becomes essential as AI capabilities mature.

Search Amazon.co.jp for "Designing Multi-Agent Systems Dibia" to access this advanced text.

Conclusion — Your Learning Roadmap

Studying AI and LLMs is an investment in future-proofing your career. The learning path matters: begin with fundamentals books to understand what AI can and can't do, then advance to prompt engineering to maximize your effectiveness with existing tools, then progressively deepen into specialized areas—whether that's LLM development, fine-tuning, or multi-agent architectures—based on your particular interests and goals.

You needn't read every book at once. Instead, assess your current level, identify your most pressing learning goals, and select the books that address them most directly. More importantly, read with your hands as much as your eyes. Immediately after studying a concept in a book, try it with Claude or ChatGPT. Test prompts in your work. Experiment with different approaches. That interplay between learning and applying transforms abstract knowledge into genuine mastery.

AI technology continues advancing rapidly, but the fundamental concepts covered in today's best books will remain relevant. By building strong conceptual foundations and practical skills now, you position yourself to adapt and thrive as the field evolves. Start with one book that matches your current interests, complete it, and build from there.

Links on this page are affiliate links. Purchases help support the ongoing operation of this site.

en.title: Bonus: Complete Guide to Deploying Next.js on Cloudflare Pages
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to deploy a Next.js app to Cloudflare Pages using Claude Code. Covers the OpenNext adapter, Wrangler CLI, environment variables, and custom domains.
en.content:
Bonus: Complete Guide to Deploying Next.js on Cloudflare Pages

Cloudflare Pages is a powerful and cost-effective platform for deploying Next.js applications. In this guide, we'll walk through the complete process of deploying a Next.js site to Cloudflare Pages using Claude Code, covering everything from setup to production.

Why Choose Cloudflare Pages?

Cloudflare Pages offers significant advantages over traditional platforms like Vercel.

Generous Free Tier

  • Unlimited static site hosting
  • 500,000 requests per month (free plan)
  • Unlimited bandwidth
  • Global delivery via Cloudflare's CDN

Edge Computing at Scale

  • Serverless functions via Cloudflare Workers
  • 300+ data centers worldwide
  • Minimal latency for content delivery
  • No geographic restrictions

Zero Cold Start Issues Unlike Vercel, where cold starts are common, Cloudflare Pages provides:

  • Instant activation via Edge Runtime
  • Always-on workers (paid plans)
  • Region-specific optimization

Enterprise-Grade Security

  • DDoS protection included by default
  • Web Application Firewall (WAF)
  • No additional configuration required

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have:

  1. Node.js (version 18.0 or higher)
  2. Cloudflare Account (free tier available)
  3. Wrangler CLI (Cloudflare's official tool)
  4. GitHub Repository (for deployment)
  5. Custom Domain (optional, claudelab.net in this example)

Install Wrangler globally:

Setting Up Your Next.js Project

If you have an existing Next.js project, you'll need to add the OpenNext adapter.

Installing the OpenNext Adapter

This adapter converts nearly all Next.js features—including App Router, API Routes, and Image Optimization—to be compatible with Cloudflare Pages.

Initialization Command

This command automatically generates:

  • open-next.config.ts — OpenNext configuration
  • wrangler.jsonc — Cloudflare configuration
  • .env.local — Environment variable template

Configuration Files Explained

open-next.config.ts

This configuration ensures all Next.js routes and APIs are converted to run on Cloudflare Workers.

wrangler.jsonc

ℹ️
`wrangler.jsonc` is JSON with comments. Wrangler natively supports .jsonc format and comments are valid.

Configuring next-intl for Multi-Language Support

Since claudelab.net supports multiple languages, configuring the next-intl middleware is essential.

i18n Middleware

⚠️
Cloudflare Pages runs on Edge Runtime, so certain APIs like `getRequestContext()` or Deno features are unavailable. Always use Node.js-compatible APIs only.

Setting i18n in next.config.js

The Build Process

Build Command

Add this to your package.json:

Build Output

On success, a .open-next directory is generated:

.open-next/
├── server/          # API Routes and SSR
├── static/          # Static files
├── cache/           # ISR cache
└── index.js         # Entry point

This entire directory is deployed to Cloudflare.

Local Development and Preview

Running with Wrangler Dev

This starts a local development server at http://localhost:8787. You can test in an environment closest to production.

ℹ️
Wrangler Dev emulates the Cloudflare Workers Edge Runtime. Even running locally, you get an environment equivalent to actual deployment.

Verifying Environment Variables

Variables defined in .env.local are automatically loaded during local development.

Deployment Methods

Method 1: Deploy with Wrangler CLI

This command:

  1. Builds the .open-next directory
  2. Uploads static files to Cloudflare
  3. Deploys the Workers script

After completion, your site is accessible at https://<your-project>.pages.dev.

Method 2: GitHub Integration (Recommended)

Deploy automatically by pushing to GitHub.

  1. Configure in Cloudflare Dashboard

    • Pages → Create → Select GitHub
    • Authorize your repository
    • Build settings: 
      Framework: Next.js
Build command: npm run build
Build output directory: .open-next

  2. Enable Auto-Deployment

    • Automatic builds on every push
    • Preview environments per PR
    • Production environment (custom domain)
ℹ️
GitHub integration automatically sets up your CI/CD pipeline. Preview environments are generated for each PR, making team review processes more efficient.

Setting Environment Variables

Via Cloudflare Dashboard

  1. Open your Pages project
  2. Navigate to Settings → Environment Variables
  3. Add variables for each environment (production/staging)
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY = sk_live_xxx
DATABASE_URL = postgresql://prod-user:pass@prod-db/prod
OPENAI_API_KEY = sk-xxx

Via Wrangler CLI

Accessing in Code

Use environment variables in API Routes and Server Components:

Setting Up a Custom Domain

Binding claudelab.net

  1. In Cloudflare Dashboard

    • Pages project → Custom Domains
    • Add claudelab.net

  2. Configure DNS

    • At your DNS provider for claudelab.net, set CNAME:
    CNAME: claudelab.net → <your-project>.pages.dev

  3. SSL/TLS Setup

    • Cloudflare automatically issues Let's Encrypt certificates
    • HTTPS is enabled by default

  4. Redirect Configuration

    http://claudelab.net → https://claudelab.net (automatic)
www.claudelab.net → https://claudelab.net (via wrangler)
⚠️
DNS changes may take up to 24 hours to propagate. However, if Cloudflare is your nameserver, propagation typically occurs within minutes.

Troubleshooting

Node.js APIs Unavailable in Edge Runtime

Cloudflare Workers runs on Edge Runtime, so some Node.js APIs are unavailable.

Unavailable Features:

  • fs (filesystem)
  • child_process (subprocess)
  • Some crypto methods
  • Direct process.env access (use different methods)

Workaround:

Image Optimization Limitations

next/image doesn't work by default, but OpenNext handles this:

Optimizing Build Size

OpenNext can split functions by route:

Claude Code Workflow

Using Claude Code automates the entire deployment process.

1. Initial Setup

Ask Claude Code to:

Integrate OpenNext and Wrangler into this Next.js project.
1. Install @opennextjs/cloudflare
2. Generate open-next.config.ts
3. Configure wrangler.jsonc
4. Update package.json build scripts

2. Build and Local Testing

1. Build Next.js with npm run build
2. Start local environment with npx wrangler dev
3. Verify all pages work before deployment

3. Pre-Deployment Checks

Claude Code verifies:

  • [ ] All environment variables are configured
  • [ ] next-intl i18n middleware is functioning
  • [ ] API Routes work on Cloudflare Workers
  • [ ] Static assets are in .open-next/static

4. Production Deployment

Claude Code automatically validates post-deployment:

  1. Custom domain (claudelab.net) is accessible

Best Practices

1. Staged Deployment

2. Cache Strategy

3. Logging and Monitoring

Use Cloudflare's logging features:

4. Version Control

Include deployment config in version control:

Summary

Cloudflare Pages is an excellent platform for deploying Next.js applications. Following this guide enables you to:

  • Host on a global CDN
  • Avoid cold start delays
  • Start free and scale affordably
  • Get automatic HTTPS and DDoS protection

By leveraging Claude Code, the entire process becomes even more efficient and error-free. Like claudelab.net, your production environments can run stably and reliably.

Happy deploying!

en.title: Claude AI Plan Comparison — Free, Pro, Max, Team & Enterprise
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Compare all Claude AI plans side by side. Pricing, features, limits, and which plan is right for you — from free to enterprise.
en.content:
Understanding Claude AI's Plan Structure

As of 2026, Claude AI offers five distinct plans designed to serve users at every scale—from individual learners to large enterprises. Each plan is tailored to specific use cases, offering different usage limits, feature sets, and price points.

This comprehensive guide walks you through each plan's capabilities, limitations, and pricing, helping you make an informed decision about which plan is right for you.

Free Plan (No Cost)

Monthly Price: Free

Overview

The Free plan is perfect for newcomers to Claude or anyone wanting to explore AI capabilities without financial commitment. While usage is limited, you get access to Claude's core functionality.

Available Features

  • Supported Models: Claude Sonnet 4.5, Haiku 4.5
  • File Uploads: Supported (PDFs, text files, images, etc.)
  • Projects Feature: Available
  • Web Search: Enabled
  • Conversation History: Full access and management

Usage Limits and Restrictions

  • Daily Message Limit: 30–100 messages per day (varies based on message complexity and time of day)
  • Reset Cycle: Messages reset every 4–8 hours
  • Priority Level: Lowest priority (limited during peak hours)
  • Unavailable Features:
    • Claude Opus model access
    • Claude Code (development environment)
    • Cowork (advanced file sharing and management)
    • Extended Thinking (complex reasoning mode)
💡
The Free plan works great for basic conversations, answering questions, and light writing tasks. Try it first to get a feel for Claude before upgrading to Pro.

Pro Plan ($20/Month)

Monthly Price: $20/month ($17/month with annual billing)

Overview

Pro is ideal for individuals, freelancers, and small teams who want to use Claude regularly. It provides 5x the daily usage of Free and unlocks powerful features like Claude Code.

Key Features

  • Available Models: Claude Opus 4.6, Opus 4.5, Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, Haiku 4.5 (user-selectable)
  • Usage Capacity: 5x Free plan limit (flexible allocation)
  • Priority Access: Higher priority during peak hours
  • Claude Code: Full IDE integration and terminal support
  • Artifacts: Code, documents, and website generation
  • Extended Thinking: Deep reasoning for complex problems
  • MCP Support: Model Context Protocol compatibility
  • Projects: Unlimited projects with full management
  • Cowork: File sharing and collaborative editing
  • Web Search: Full search capabilities
  • File Uploads: Comprehensive support

Ideal For

  • Daily Claude users working on writing, coding, and research
  • Anyone needing Opus model performance for complex problem-solving
  • Developers looking to leverage Claude Code for development acceleration
ℹ️
Pro offers the best balance of capability and price for individual users. At $20/month, you get nearly all practical features you'd need.

Max Plans — Max 5 and Max 20

The Max tier is designed for power users and developers requiring high-volume processing and advanced capabilities. Two options are available.

Max 5 Plan

Monthly Price: $100/month

Key Specifications

  • Usage Capacity: 20x Pro plan (100x Free plan)
  • Priority Level: Very high (minimal restrictions)
  • Available Models: All models including Opus 4.6
  • Claude Code: Full-featured development environment
  • Additional Features (beyond Pro):
    • Research capabilities (advanced investigation tools)
    • Cowork (full advanced features)

Best For

  • Developers using Claude Code daily
  • Large-scale data analysis and processing
  • API-integrated projects with high request volumes

Max 20 Plan

Monthly Price: $200/month

Key Specifications

  • Usage Capacity: 80x Pro plan (400x Free plan)
  • Priority Level: Highest priority (zero-latency)
  • Available Models: All models
  • Claude Code: Full-featured development environment
  • Additional Features: Everything in Max 5, plus maximum performance

Best For

  • Large-scale AI development teams
  • Heavy-use automation systems
  • Projects requiring constant, high-frequency processing
💡
Claude Code is available exclusively on Pro and above (Pro, Max 5, Max 20). If IDE-based development is essential, Pro is your minimum requirement. Choose between Max 5 and Max 20 based on your monthly usage patterns and latency requirements.

Team Plan ($25/User/Month)

Monthly Price: $25 per user per month (or $30/month annual billing variations apply)

Overview

Team is built for organizations where multiple people collaborate on Claude projects. A minimum of five team members is required, with unified management capabilities.

Team Management Features

  • User Administration: Centralized control over team member accounts
  • Shared Projects: Collaborate on projects across your entire team
  • Admin Dashboard: Usage monitoring, access controls, and billing management
  • Priority Support: Dedicated support channel for your team

Security and Compliance

  • Data Privacy Guarantee: Team conversations are never used to train Anthropic's next-generation models
  • Encrypted Communications: End-to-end protection for all chats
  • Audit Logs: Complete record of access and activity

Usage Capacity

Each team member receives Pro-equivalent usage limits. The admin dashboard provides aggregated usage analytics across the entire team.

Ideal For

  • Multi-member teams wanting unified Claude deployment
  • Organizations needing security and governance controls
  • Projects where data privacy and compliance matter
ℹ️
Team provides secure, managed access to Claude with team-wide governance. If your organization handles sensitive data or requires access controls, Team or Enterprise is the right choice.

Enterprise Plan (Custom Pricing)

Monthly Price: Custom (contact sales)

Overview

Enterprise is tailored for large organizations with specific requirements around security, compliance, scale, and integration. Pricing and features are customized through direct negotiation with Anthropic's sales team.

Key Characteristics

  • Custom Pricing: Based on organization size, usage volume, and feature needs
  • Custom Usage Capacity: Scales to meet your organization's demands
  • All Models Available: Full access to the complete Claude model lineup

Advanced Security Features

  • SSO / SAML Authentication: Enterprise-grade identity management
  • Comprehensive Audit Logs: Detailed activity tracking for compliance
  • Service Level Agreements (SLAs): Performance and uptime guarantees
  • Custom Data Retention Policies: Define how long data is stored
  • Regional Data Compliance: GDPR, HIPAA, and other regulatory support

Support and Services

  • Dedicated Support Team: 24/7 professional support
  • Custom Onboarding: Tailored training for your organization
  • Technical Consulting: Expert guidance on implementation best practices

Customization Options

  • Fine-Tuned Models: Adjust Claude to match your organization's specific needs
  • API Integration: Seamless connection with existing systems
  • White-Label Options: Customize the interface for your organization

Ideal For

  • Fortune 500 companies and large enterprises
  • Healthcare, finance, and government sectors
  • Organizations with strict compliance requirements (HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2)
  • Projects requiring custom model tuning or specialized features
💡
To discuss Enterprise pricing and features, contact Anthropic's sales team at sales@anthropic.com. They'll work with you to design the perfect plan for your organization's needs.

Plan Selection Decision Tree

Use this flowchart to identify your ideal plan:

Step 1: Define Your Usage Pattern

How many people will use Claude?

  • Just me → Continue to Step 2
  • Multiple people / team / organization → Team or Enterprise

Step 2: For Individual Users — Assess Daily Usage

How frequently do you use Claude?

  • Light usage (1–2 times per week) → Free plan is sufficient
  • Regular usage (daily, 10+ messages) → Pro plan recommended
  • Heavy usage (100+ messages daily) → Max plan recommended

Step 3: Consider Required Features

Do you need Claude Code (IDE and development tools)?

  • No (text, research, writing only) → Free or Pro
  • Yes (code generation, development) → Pro or above required

Do you need Opus model (highest performance)?

  • No (Sonnet is sufficient) → Free or Pro
  • Yes (complex problem-solving) → Pro or Max

Step 4: Evaluate Priority and Performance

Can you tolerate usage limits during peak hours?

  • Yes → Free plan works
  • No → Pro or above

Do you need guaranteed fastest response times?

  • No → Pro is sufficient
  • Yes (machine learning, time-sensitive) → Max recommended

Feature Comparison Table

| Feature | Free | Pro | Max 5 | Max 20 | Team | Enterprise | |---------|------|-----|-------|--------|------|------------| | Monthly Price | Free | $20 | $100 | $200 | $25/user | Custom | | Usage Capacity | 1x | 5x | 100x | 400x | 5x/user | Custom | | Opus 4.6 | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Sonnet | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Claude Code | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Artifacts | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Extended Thinking | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Projects | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (shared) | ✅ | | Web Search | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | File Uploads | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Cowork | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Priority Level | Low | Medium | High | Highest | Medium | Custom | | Team Features | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Admin Dashboard | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | SSO / SAML | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Audit Logs | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | SLA Guarantee | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Dedicated Support | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What's the main difference between Pro and Max?

A: Three key differences:

  1. Usage Capacity: Pro gives you 5x Free limits; Max 5 gives you 100x. This determines your monthly message and token allowance.

  2. Priority and Performance: Max includes near-zero wait times and priority during peak hours. Pro may experience minor delays.

  3. Features: Both include Claude Code, but Max 5 adds advanced Research tools and enhanced Cowork features.

Bottom Line: Use Pro for regular daily work. Upgrade to Max if you're a heavy user, run automation projects, or need guaranteed performance.

Q: Are there discounts for annual billing?

A: Yes:

  • Pro: $20/month → $17/month (annual) = ~15% savings
  • Max: Check current rates for annual discounts
  • Team: Annual pricing may offer better rates than month-to-month

Check the official pricing page for the latest discount details.

Q: Do you offer student discounts?

A: Currently, no public student discount is listed. However, Anthropic may have educational programs available. Contact support@anthropic.com to inquire about academic pricing.

Q: How does Claude.ai differ from the Claude API?

A: Major differences:

  • Claude.ai (Web Interface): Fixed monthly pricing (Free, Pro, Max, Team) with monthly usage limits
  • Claude API: Pay-as-you-go based on tokens consumed, ideal for high-volume automated systems

For small projects, Claude.ai Pro is more economical. For large-scale automation, the API's pay-per-use model may be cheaper. Compare the two based on your expected token usage.

Q: Can I switch plans easily?

A: Yes, absolutely. Plan changes are instant:

  • Upgrading (Free → Pro, Pro → Max): Immediate activation
  • Downgrading: Takes effect at the start of your next billing cycle

You can switch monthly, making it easy to test Pro before committing.

Q: Is Team plan required for group usage?

A: Not required, but highly recommended if:

  • You should use Team if:

    • Your team needs shared projects and collaboration
    • You handle sensitive company data
    • You need security controls and audit trails
    • You want centralized billing for multiple users

  • Individual Pro plans work if:

    • Each person has their own Free/Pro account
    • You only share final outputs
    • Security requirements are minimal

Choose based on your team's size, sensitivity of data, and governance needs.

Q: How much does Enterprise cost?

A: Enterprise pricing is completely custom and depends on:

  • Organization size
  • Required usage volume (tokens/month)
  • Security and compliance requirements
  • SLA needs
  • Custom feature requests

For a quote, contact Anthropic's sales team: sales@anthropic.com

Q: Are there free trial periods?

A: The Free plan is free indefinitely. Pro and Max require payment upon signup.

New users sometimes receive small welcome credits (a few dollars) to test paid plans. Check your account after signing up.

Q: What about usage overages?

A: Claude.ai plans don't have overage fees. Once you hit your limit, you simply wait for the next reset period. For guaranteed capacity, upgrade to a higher tier. The API charges for actual usage without monthly caps.

Q: Can I integrate Claude into my own app?

A: Yes, use the Claude API. Claude.ai plans are for the web interface. The API is separate with its own pay-as-you-go pricing. See the Claude API documentation for integration details.

Plan Selection Quick Reference

| Use Case | Recommended Plan | Why | |----------|-----------------|-----| | Learning and exploration | Free | Test Claude at no cost | | Daily personal use | Pro | Best value for regular use | | Professional writing and analysis | Pro | All features needed, excellent price | | Software development | Pro or Max | Claude Code essential, Max for heavy workloads | | Heavy automation projects | Max | High usage capacity, best performance | | Multi-person teams | Team | Built for collaboration and governance | | Enterprise deployment | Enterprise | Security, compliance, custom features |

Getting Started

Ready to choose your plan? Here's what we recommend:

  1. Start with Free to explore Claude's capabilities
  2. Upgrade to Pro when you're using it daily ($20/month is a small investment for a powerful tool)
  3. Consider Max if you're a developer or heavy user
  4. Contact sales for Team or Enterprise if you're part of an organization

For the latest pricing information and feature updates, visit the official Claude AI pricing page.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Can I use Opus on the Free plan?

No, Opus models (Opus 4.6 and 4.5) are exclusively available on Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans. Free plan users can access Claude Sonnet and Haiku models. If you need Opus's superior reasoning capabilities, upgrade to Pro for $20/month.

What's the difference between Pro and Max?

Pro ($20/month) provides 5x Free's usage capacity with access to Claude Code and all core features. Max 5 ($100/month) provides 20x Pro's capacity with advanced Research tools and priority support. Choose Pro for regular daily work; choose Max for heavy automation, large-scale analysis, or guaranteed performance.

Can individuals use the Team plan?

Team plan is designed for organizations with minimum 5 members. Individual users should use Free, Pro, or Max plans instead. Team provides shared project management, audit logs, and enterprise security features tailored for multi-person collaboration.

How long are Claude conversations kept in my account?

Conversation history is stored indefinitely on Free, Pro, and Max plans. You can access past conversations anytime. Team and Enterprise plans allow custom data retention policies. You can delete individual conversations or your entire chat history manually.

What happens if I exceed my plan's usage limit?

Free and Pro plans have daily/monthly message limits. Once you reach your limit, you'll need to wait for the reset period (4-8 hours for Free). Pro doesn't allow overage purchasing; upgrade to Max for higher capacity. Max plans have very high limits rarely exceeded in typical use.

Can I switch between plans mid-month?

Yes, you can upgrade or downgrade anytime. Upgrades take effect immediately. Downgrades apply at your next billing cycle, and you won't be charged extra. This flexibility lets you test higher tiers before full commitment.

References and Further Reading

  • Claude AI Official Pricing
  • Claude API Documentation
  • Anthropic Blog

en.title: Claude API Error Handling and Retry Strategies
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn about Claude API error codes, retry strategies, and rate limit handling. Best practices for building robust API integrations in production.
en.content:
Claude API Error Handling and Retry Strategies

Building robust and reliable API integrations requires understanding how to properly handle errors and implement effective retry strategies. This comprehensive guide covers error types that can occur with the Claude API, how to handle them appropriately, and best practices for production environments.

HTTP Status Codes and Error Responses

Understanding the HTTP status codes returned by the Claude API and how to respond to each is fundamental to robust error handling.

HTTP Status Codes Reference

| Status Code | Name | Description | Response | |---|---|---|---| | 400 | Bad Request | Invalid request parameters | Validate and fix the request | | 401 | Unauthorized | Missing or invalid API key | Check API key configuration | | 403 | Forbidden | Request operation not permitted | Verify account permissions | | 404 | Not Found | Requested resource doesn't exist | Check endpoint or model ID | | 429 | Too Many Requests | Rate limit exceeded | Implement backoff and retry | | 500 | Internal Server Error | Server-side error occurred | Retry with exponential backoff | | 529 | Service Unavailable | Server is overloaded | Retry with longer backoff |

Standard Error Response Format

All error responses from the Claude API follow a standardized JSON format:

Example error response:

Error Types and Handling Strategies

Error Type Reference

| Error Type | HTTP Status | Description | Recommended Action | |---|---|---|---| | invalid_request_error | 400 | Invalid request parameters | Fix code, do not retry | | authentication_error | 401 | Authentication failed | Verify API key | | permission_error | 403 | Operation not permitted | Check account permissions | | not_found_error | 404 | Resource not found | Verify resource ID | | rate_limit_error | 429 | Rate limit exceeded | Backoff and retry | | api_error | 500 | Server internal error | Retry with backoff | | overloaded_error | 529 | Server overloaded | Retry with longer backoff |

Error Handling Decision Flow

Critical Note: Errors 400, 401, 403, and 404 indicate code issues and won't succeed on retry. Always fix the code before retrying.

Understanding Rate Limiting

The Claude API implements rate limiting to prevent excessive requests and ensure fair resource allocation.

Rate Limit Parameters

  • RPM (Requests Per Minute): Maximum number of requests allowed per minute
  • TPM (Tokens Per Minute): Maximum number of tokens processed per minute

When rate limits are exceeded, the API returns HTTP 429 with a rate_limit_error.

Response headers contain rate limit information:

RateLimit-Limit-Requests: 50
RateLimit-Limit-Tokens: 90000
RateLimit-Remaining-Requests: 10
RateLimit-Remaining-Tokens: 45000
RateLimit-Reset-Requests: 2026-03-08T12:34:56Z
RateLimit-Reset-Tokens: 2026-03-08T12:34:56Z

Monitoring these headers helps you plan retries more effectively and avoid hitting rate limits.

Implementing Retry Strategies

Exponential Backoff Fundamentals

Retries should never be unlimited. Exponential backoff reduces server load while improving your chances of success:

wait_time = min(base_delay * (2 ^ retry_count), max_delay) + jitter

The jitter (random component) prevents thundering herd problems where many clients retry simultaneously.

Python Implementation

Here's how to implement exponential backoff in Python:

TypeScript Implementation

Here's the equivalent implementation in TypeScript:

SDK Built-in Retry Behavior

The Anthropic Python and TypeScript SDKs include automatic retry functionality out of the box.

Python SDK

The Python SDK automatically retries in these scenarios:

  • HTTP 429 (Rate Limit Error)
  • HTTP 500 (Internal Server Error)
  • HTTP 529 (Service Unavailable)

The default maximum retry attempts is 3:

You can customize retry behavior by passing retry configuration:

TypeScript SDK

The TypeScript SDK provides similar automatic retry behavior:

Tip: While SDK auto-retry is convenient, custom retry logic is useful for complex requirements, additional context handling, or specific retry policies not covered by the SDK.

Circuit Breaker Pattern

The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service. When too many errors occur, the breaker "opens" and prevents further requests temporarily.

Python Circuit Breaker Implementation

TypeScript Circuit Breaker Implementation

Handling Streaming Errors

Streaming responses require different error handling approaches compared to standard requests.

Streaming-Specific Errors

  • Connection Errors: Network connection is lost or interrupted
  • Stream Event Errors: Errors occur during streaming event processing
  • Timeout Errors: Timeout waiting for stream data

Python Streaming Error Handling

TypeScript Streaming Error Handling

Production Best Practices

1. Set Request Timeouts

Prevent requests from hanging indefinitely:

2. Implement Comprehensive Error Logging

Log errors with full context for debugging:

3. Identify Retryable Errors Correctly

Not all errors should be retried:

4. Request Cancellation Support

Allow long-running requests to be cancelled:

Troubleshooting Guide

Common Issues and Solutions

Problem: Frequent 429 Rate Limit Errors

  • Increase backoff wait times
  • Reduce request frequency
  • Increase maximum retry attempts
  • Batch requests when possible
  • Monitor RPM and TPM headers

Problem: Persistent 500 Errors

  • Check API status page at status.anthropic.com
  • Contact support with request IDs
  • Verify API key has necessary permissions
  • Check for API deprecations

Problem: Timeout Errors

  • Increase timeout values gradually
  • Simplify requests (shorter prompts)
  • Reduce max_tokens value
  • Check network connectivity
  • Verify server latency

Problem: Streaming Disconnections

  • Implement automatic reconnection logic
  • Check for client-side network issues
  • Verify connection stability
  • Use connection keep-alives

Summary

Effective error handling and retry strategies are fundamental to building production-grade API integrations. By implementing the patterns and practices covered in this guide, you can create resilient systems that gracefully handle transient failures while avoiding unnecessary strain on the API infrastructure.

Key takeaways:

  • Understand different error types and handle each appropriately
  • Implement exponential backoff with jitter for retries
  • Use circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures
  • Handle streaming errors with special consideration
  • Log errors comprehensively for debugging
  • Monitor rate limit headers proactively
  • Set appropriate timeouts for all requests
  • Test error handling paths thoroughly

For more information and updates, refer to the official Claude API documentation.

en.title: Claude Code Agent Teams — Accelerate Development with Multi-AI Coordination
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude Code's subagent system and agent teams. From parallel task processing and custom agent creation to batch operations, discover techniques for coordinating multiple AI agents in your development workflow.
en.content:
Understanding Agent Teams in Claude Code

Modern development workflows often require juggling multiple tasks simultaneously: refactoring code while reviewing pull requests, writing tests while documenting API changes, or analyzing performance across different modules. Claude Code's agent team system lets you distribute these tasks across specialized AI agents that work in parallel, coordinating through a main orchestrating agent.

At the core of this system is the subagent concept. When your main Claude Code session encounters a complex task, it can spawn specialized subagents to handle specific responsibilities. Rather than the main agent trying to do everything sequentially, it breaks work into logical chunks and coordinates multiple agents working in parallel. This not only speeds up development but also allows each agent to specialize in what it does best.

Agent Types and Specialization

Claude Code provides several predefined agent types, each designed for specific use cases in your workflow:

Explore agents operate in read-only mode, making them perfect for investigation and analysis tasks. They can examine your codebase, search for patterns, review documentation, and provide insights without risking unintended modifications. Use these when you need comprehensive code analysis or when you want to verify assumptions before making changes.

Plan agents focus on strategic thinking and decision-making. These agents excel at architecture review, refactoring strategy, migration planning, and creating implementation roadmaps. They combine analysis with forward-looking recommendations.

General-purpose agents handle the full spectrum of development tasks, from writing code to refactoring to documentation updates. They're your go-to choice when you need flexibility and don't need specialization.

Bash agents specialize in command-line operations and scripting. They're essential for deployment tasks, environment setup, performance testing, and any work that requires direct shell access. Their specialization makes them faster and more reliable for command-heavy workflows.

Creating Custom Subagents

Beyond the built-in types, you can define custom subagents tailored to your project's specific needs. Store agent definitions in a .claude/agents/ directory within your workspace. Each agent is defined as a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter:

This custom agent inherits the tools you specify and can be invoked alongside your other agents. You define what tools it has access to, what model powers it, and its specialized instructions through the system prompt.

Dynamic Agent Definition with --agents Flag

For workflows where agent composition changes based on the task at hand, use the --agents flag to define agents dynamically:

This approach lets you compose agent teams on-the-fly without predefined configuration. The specified agents spin up, each review the codebase from their specialty angle, and coordinate findings back through the main agent.

Parallel Processing at Scale: The /batch Skill

When you need to apply consistent changes across a large codebase, the /batch skill orchestrates parallel operations using temporary git worktrees. Rather than applying changes sequentially (which would be slow), /batch creates isolated working trees for each task, allowing multiple transformations to happen simultaneously:

This might create five parallel worktrees, each handling different modules. As each worktree completes its task, changes merge back to your main branch. The parallelization dramatically reduces execution time for large-scale refactoring initiatives.

Multi-Agent Review with /simplify

Code simplification often benefits from multiple perspectives. The /simplify skill automatically spawns three parallel review agents, each analyzing code from a different angle—one focusing on readability, another on performance, and a third on maintainability. They work simultaneously, then coordinate their feedback for a comprehensive simplification strategy.

Agent Team Configuration and Coordination Modes

Control how your agent team communicates and coordinates using the --teammate-mode flag:

  • auto mode lets Claude Code choose the best coordination method based on your system and task complexity
  • in-process mode runs all agents within your current session, useful for lightweight tasks or constrained environments
  • tmux mode creates separate tmux sessions for each agent, providing isolation and independent execution

In tmux mode, each agent operates in its own session with independent terminal state, making it ideal for tasks requiring clean separation or different environment variables.

Practical Applications: Real-World Examples

Consider a scenario where you're refactoring a large microservice. Rather than doing this sequentially, spawn specialized agents: one focusing on API contract changes, another on database migrations, and a third on testing updates. While one agent modifies the API layer, another can simultaneously work on the data model, and the third crafts comprehensive tests. The main agent coordinates progress, resolves conflicts, and ensures consistency.

Another common workflow involves simultaneous PR review and test expansion. When a colleague submits a pull request, launch a review agent to analyze code quality while spawning another agent to identify untested code paths and draft additional test cases. Both work in parallel, and within minutes you have comprehensive review feedback plus expanded test coverage—something that would take much longer sequentially.

Large-scale code migration projects particularly benefit from agent teams. You might spawn agents to migrate different modules to a new framework simultaneously. Each agent operates independently, encounters module-specific challenges, and learns from attempts. The main agent coordinates shared concerns like shared utilities and ensures the final result cohesively fits together.

Integration with the Agent SDK

Claude Code's agent team system builds on top of the Claude Agent SDK, providing a high-level interface for multi-agent coordination. If you need deeper customization or want to build agent teams programmatically, the SDK offers API-level integration:

Through the SDK, you can build custom orchestration logic, integrate agent teams into automated pipelines, and create sophisticated multi-agent workflows tailored to your specific development process.

Getting Started with Agent Teams

Start by identifying workflow bottlenecks where sequential processing slows you down. Could code review happen in parallel with test expansion? Could architectural planning happen while implementation begins on separate modules? These are prime candidates for agent teams.

Begin with predefined agent types, then experiment with custom agents as you discover specialty needs in your workflow. The --agents flag provides flexibility for exploration without requiring permanent configuration. As your team scales, invest time in defining reusable custom agents that encode best practices for your organization.

Agent teams transform development from a sequential process into a coordinated, parallel effort. When used strategically, they accelerate iteration cycles, improve code quality through multiple perspectives, and let you accomplish in hours what once took days.

en.title: Claude Code CI/CD Integration Guide — GitHub Actions
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to integrate Claude Code with GitHub Actions for automated PR reviews, issue handling, and code generation.
en.content:
Claude Code CI/CD Integration Guide — GitHub Actions

Integrating Claude Code with GitHub Actions enables you to automate code reviews, respond to issues, and generate code directly within your GitHub workflows. This guide covers both quick setup and advanced configurations for production environments.

Overview

Claude Code GitHub Actions integration allows you to:

  • Automated PR Reviews: Use Claude to review pull requests and provide intelligent feedback
  • Issue Handling: Automatically respond to issues and generate solutions
  • Code Generation: Create code based on issue descriptions or comments
  • Intelligent Comments: Mention @claude in PR comments to trigger analysis
  • Multi-Model Support: Leverage AWS Bedrock and Google Vertex AI integrations

The integration uses the official anthropics/claude-code-action@v1 GitHub Action, which supports multiple triggers including pull requests, issue comments, and pull request review comments.

Quick Setup with Claude CLI

The fastest way to get started is using the Claude CLI's installation command. This automates most of the configuration for you.

Prerequisites

  • GitHub repository with admin access
  • Anthropic API key (from console.anthropic.com)
  • Claude CLI installed on your machine

Installation Steps

Run the quick setup command:

This command will:

  1. Automatically install the Claude GitHub App to your repository
  2. Create the required ANTHROPIC_API_KEY secret
  3. Set up a basic workflow file with sensible defaults
  4. Configure repository permissions for Contents, Issues, and Pull Requests

After running this command, the integration is immediately active and ready to respond to PR reviews, issue comments, and pull request review comments.

Tip: The quick setup uses default parameters optimized for most projects. For advanced configurations, refer to the manual setup section.

Manual Setup

If you prefer more control over the configuration or need to troubleshoot, follow these manual setup steps.

Step 1: Install the Claude GitHub App

  1. Visit the Claude GitHub App installation page
  2. Select your GitHub account or organization
  3. Choose the repositories where you want to use Claude Code
  4. Grant the required permissions (see Permissions section below)
  5. Complete the installation

Step 2: Add the API Key Secret

  1. Go to your repository settings
  2. Navigate to Secrets and variables → Actions
  3. Click New repository secret
  4. Name it ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
  5. Paste your Anthropic API key from console.anthropic.com
  6. Click Add secret

Security Note: Never commit your API key to your repository. Always use GitHub secrets for sensitive credentials.

Step 3: Create the Workflow File

Create a new file in your repository at .github/workflows/claude-code.yml:

Commit this file to your repository's main branch to activate the integration.

Repository Permissions

The Claude Code GitHub Action requires specific permissions to function properly. Configure these in your workflow file or repository settings.

| Permission | Level | Purpose | |-----------|-------|---------| | Contents | Read/Write | Access and modify repository files for analysis and code generation | | Issues | Read/Write | Comment on issues and create issue labels | | Pull Requests | Read/Write | Review PRs, post comments, and suggest changes | | Discussions | Read/Write | Optional: Participate in repository discussions |

Ensure your workflow includes the correct permissions block:

Workflow Triggers

The Claude Code Action responds to multiple trigger types. Understanding these helps you optimize when Claude engages with your repository.

Trigger Types

Issue Comments (issue_comment)

  • Activated when someone comments on an issue
  • Use @claude mentions to request analysis or code generation
  • Great for follow-up discussions and clarifications

Pull Request Review Comments (pull_request_review_comment)

  • Triggered by comments on PR diffs
  • Allows Claude to respond to inline code suggestions
  • Enables targeted feedback on specific code sections

Pull Request Events (pull_request)

  • Runs when PRs are opened or updated
  • Useful for automatic PR reviews
  • Can trigger on specific branches using branches filter

Example: Conditional Triggers

Configuration Parameters

The Claude Code Action supports multiple parameters to customize behavior and capabilities.

Parameter Reference Table

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description | |-----------|------|---------|-------------| | anthropic_api_key | string | — | Your Anthropic API key (use secrets) | | prompt | string | — | Custom system prompt for Claude | | claude_args | string | --max-turns=3 | Additional Claude CLI arguments | | trigger_phrase | string | @claude | Word/phrase to trigger Claude's response | | use_bedrock | boolean | false | Use AWS Bedrock for model inference | | use_vertex | boolean | false | Use Google Vertex AI for model inference | | max_turns | integer | 3 | Maximum conversation turns allowed |

Parameter Examples

With AWS Bedrock:

With Google Vertex AI:

Custom System Prompt:

Common Use Cases

Automated PR Reviews

Automatically review PRs for code quality, security issues, and best practices:

Issue Response Automation

Automatically respond to new issues with analysis and solution suggestions:

Code Generation from Issues

Generate code implementations based on feature requests:

The /review Skill

The /review skill provides specialized PR review capabilities when mentioned in comments:

@claude /review

When invoked, Claude will:

  • Analyze all files changed in the PR
  • Provide detailed feedback on each change
  • Suggest improvements and alternatives
  • Check for potential bugs and edge cases
  • Verify adherence to project standards

Using /review Effectively

Comment on a PR with:

@claude /review --focus=security,performance

Additional focus options:

  • security: Check for vulnerabilities
  • performance: Identify optimization opportunities
  • tests: Evaluate test coverage
  • documentation: Review code documentation
  • all: Comprehensive review (default)

PR Creation from Issues

Claude can create pull requests directly from issues when requested:

@claude /create-pr "Implement user authentication system"

This workflow:

  1. Creates a feature branch from the issue
  2. Generates code based on issue requirements
  3. Creates a pull request with the generated code
  4. Links the PR back to the original issue
  5. Runs initial tests and checks

Environment-Specific Configuration

Development Workflow

Production Review Workflow

Best Practices

1. Use a CLAUDE.md File

Create a CLAUDE.md file in your repository root to provide context:

Claude will reference this file to maintain consistency with your project standards.

2. Never Commit Secrets

Always use GitHub secrets for API keys:

3. Control Turn Limits

Set appropriate --max-turns values to control API costs:

4. Use Concurrency Controls

Prevent multiple runs from conflicting:

5. Monitor API Usage

Log Claude's activity for monitoring:

6. Implement Rate Limiting

Protect your API quota:

7. Use Conditional Workflows

Only run Claude on relevant changes:

Troubleshooting

Claude Not Responding

Issue: Comments with @claude mentions aren't triggering responses.

Solutions:

  • Verify ANTHROPIC_API_KEY secret is set correctly
  • Check that the workflow file is on the default branch
  • Ensure workflow permissions include issues: write and pull-requests: write
  • Check GitHub Actions is enabled in repository settings

API Key Errors

Issue: "Invalid API key" or "Authentication failed" errors.

Solutions:

  • Confirm the API key is active on console.anthropic.com
  • Verify the secret name matches exactly in the workflow
  • Regenerate the API key if unsure of validity
  • Check for accidental spaces or special characters

Rate Limiting

Issue: Claude frequently hits rate limits.

Solutions:

  • Reduce --max-turns to lower API calls
  • Implement workflow concurrency controls
  • Add delays between trigger conditions
  • Consider using Bedrock for higher limits

Permission Errors

Issue: "Insufficient permissions" errors in logs.

Solutions:

  • Verify permissions block in workflow file
  • Check organization-level restrictions
  • Ensure app has necessary GitHub permissions
  • Review branch protection rule configurations

Advanced Integration Patterns

Multi-Model Comparison

Use different models for different purposes:

Multi-Cloud Deployment

Leverage different infrastructure providers:

Monitoring and Analytics

Tracking Claude Activity

Monitor how Claude is being used:

Measuring Impact

Track metrics like:

  • Average time from issue to PR creation
  • Code review turnaround time
  • Bug detection rate improvement
  • Developer satisfaction scores

Conclusion

Claude Code's GitHub Actions integration brings AI-powered automation to your development workflow. Start with the quick setup for immediate benefits, then explore advanced configurations as your team's needs evolve.

Remember to:

  • Keep API keys secure
  • Monitor API usage and costs
  • Provide clear context with CLAUDE.md
  • Test configurations in development branches
  • Review Claude's output before merging

For more information, visit the Claude documentation or GitHub repository.

en.title: Claude Code IDE Integration Guide — VS Code & JetBrains
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude Code's VS Code extension and JetBrains integration. Covers setup, file referencing, permission modes, and shortcuts.
en.content:
Claude Code IDE Integration Guide — VS Code & JetBrains

Claude Code transforms your development workflow by integrating AI-powered assistance directly into your favorite IDEs. This comprehensive guide walks you through setting up and mastering Claude Code integration in both VS Code and JetBrains IDEs.

Overview

Claude Code provides seamless integration with modern development environments, allowing you to leverage AI capabilities without leaving your IDE. Whether you're using VS Code or a JetBrains IDE, you can access Claude's advanced reasoning, code analysis, and generation features directly within your editor.

Key Benefits

  • Contextual Understanding: Claude understands your code, project structure, and files in context
  • Multi-File References: Reference multiple files, folders, and specific line ranges in your conversations
  • Permission Control: Fine-grained control over what Claude can access and modify
  • Keyboard-Driven Workflow: Optimized shortcuts for efficient development
  • MCP Integration: Extend Claude's capabilities with Model Context Protocol servers
  • Multi-Session Support: Manage multiple independent conversations simultaneously

VS Code Extension Setup

Installation and Requirements

The Claude Code extension for VS Code is available on the VS Code Marketplace and officially maintained by Anthropic.

Requirements:

  • VS Code 1.98.0 or later
  • Active Claude account with API access
  • Internet connection for API communication

Installation Steps:

  1. Open VS Code
  2. Navigate to the Extensions marketplace (Cmd+Shift+X on macOS, Ctrl+Shift+X on Windows/Linux)
  3. Search for "Claude Code" by Anthropic
  4. Click "Install" on the official extension (ID: vscode:extension/anthropic.claude-code)
  5. Follow the authentication prompts to connect your Claude account
  6. The extension will initialize and display the Claude panel

Accessing Claude Code

Once installed, you can access Claude Code through multiple entry points:

Spark Icon in Activity Bar The Claude Code icon appears in the left-hand Activity Bar. Click it to open the Claude panel with your current conversations and conversation history.

Command Palette

  • Press Cmd+Shift+P (macOS) or Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows/Linux)
  • Search for "Claude Code" to see available commands
  • Common commands include "New Conversation", "Focus Claude", and "Reference File"

Status Bar The status bar at the bottom of your window displays Claude Code status. Click the Claude indicator to quickly access the extension features.

Context Menu Right-click on files, folders, or selected code to quickly reference them in Claude Code with contextual menu options.

Configuration Settings

Claude Code extension provides several configurable settings to customize your experience. Access settings via VS Code's Settings interface (Cmd+, on macOS).

| Setting | Type | Default | Description | |---------|------|---------|-------------| | claude-code.selectedModel | string | claude-opus-4-6 | The Claude model to use for conversations (e.g., claude-opus-4-6, claude-sonnet-4-20250514) | | claude-code.useTerminal | boolean | true | Enable integrated terminal access for Claude to execute commands | | claude-code.initialPermissionMode | enum | Normal | Default permission mode: Normal, Plan, or Auto-Accept | | claude-code.preferredLocation | enum | Side Panel | Where Claude panel appears: Side Panel, Bottom Panel, or Fullscreen | | claude-code.autosave | boolean | true | Automatically save changes made by Claude | | claude-code.respectGitIgnore | boolean | true | Respect .gitignore rules when referencing files |

Example Configuration (settings.json):

Pro Tip: Set initialPermissionMode to "Plan" if you want Claude to preview changes before applying them. This is useful when learning or working on critical code.

File Referencing in Claude Code

One of Claude Code's most powerful features is the ability to reference files, folders, and specific sections of your codebase directly in conversations.

File Reference Syntax

Claude Code uses the @ symbol to reference files and folders. This works similar to mentions in collaborative tools.

Single File Reference

@app.ts
@src/utils/helper.js
@package.json

Claude will include the entire file contents in the conversation context.

Folder References

@src/components/
@utils/
@tests/

When referencing a folder, Claude includes all files in that directory, respecting your .gitignore rules if enabled.

Specific Line Ranges

@app.ts#5-10
@src/api/client.ts#1-25
@middleware.js#45-60

Reference specific lines within a file to focus Claude's attention on particular code sections.

PDF Document Pages

@document.pdf#1-10
@api-spec.pdf#15-20
@architecture.pdf

Claude can analyze PDF documents and specific page ranges, useful for referencing API specifications or architectural diagrams.

Fuzzy File Search

@auth
@component
@database

Use fuzzy matching to quickly find files without typing the full path. Claude will suggest matching files as you type.

File Reference Workflow

  1. Start typing @ in the Claude Code input field
  2. Claude displays matching files and folders
  3. Select the desired file or folder
  4. Claude inserts the reference and highlights it
  5. Send your message—Claude includes the referenced files in the context

This workflow ensures Claude has the necessary context to provide accurate, code-aware assistance.

Permission Modes

Claude Code implements a permission system to give you control over what Claude can do with your files and environment.

Permission Mode Overview

| Mode | Description | Use Case | |------|-------------|----------| | Normal | Claude shows proposed changes and waits for your approval before applying them | Default mode; recommended for critical changes | | Plan | Claude presents a detailed plan and asks for confirmation before executing actions | Learning mode; understand what Claude will do | | Auto-Accept | Claude automatically applies changes without asking for approval | Trusted scenarios; productivity-focused workflow |

Setting Permission Modes

Global Setting: Configure the default permission mode in VS Code settings:

claude-code.initialPermissionMode: "Normal"

Per-Conversation Override: You can change the permission mode for individual conversations within the Claude panel by selecting the desired mode from the dropdown menu.

Security Note: Use "Auto-Accept" cautiously. This mode allows Claude to modify files without approval. Only enable it when working in isolated projects or after verifying Claude's recent changes.

Keyboard Shortcuts

Efficient keyboard navigation is essential for maintaining flow during development. Claude Code provides several optimized shortcuts.

Essential Shortcuts

| Shortcut | macOS | Windows/Linux | Action | |----------|-------|---------------|--------| | Focus Claude | Cmd+Esc | Ctrl+Esc | Move focus to Claude panel from editor | | New Conversation | Cmd+N | Ctrl+N | Start a new Claude conversation | | @ Mention | Option+K | Alt+K | Open file/folder selector for quick references | | Search Files | Cmd+P | Ctrl+P | Quick file search (then @ to reference) | | Send Message | Enter | Enter | Send message in Claude input field | | Clear Context | Cmd+Shift+L | Ctrl+Shift+L | Clear conversation history and context |

Navigation Tips

  • Press Cmd+Esc (or Ctrl+Esc) from any editor position to immediately focus the Claude panel
  • Use Option+K (or Alt+K) to bring up the file selector without typing @
  • Combine Cmd+P with @ to search and reference files in a single operation

Multi-Session Support

Claude Code supports multiple independent conversations simultaneously, each in a separate tab within the Claude panel.

Creating New Sessions

  1. Click the "+" icon at the top of the Claude panel
  2. Or press Cmd+N (or Ctrl+N)
  3. A new conversation tab opens with fresh context
  4. Each session maintains its own conversation history and file references

Session Management

  • Switch Between Sessions: Click conversation tabs to switch between open sessions
  • Close Sessions: Click the X on a tab to close that conversation
  • Session Persistence: Conversation history is automatically saved and persists across VS Code restarts
  • Independent Context: Each session maintains separate file references and conversation state

This multi-session capability allows you to work on different tasks simultaneously without losing context.

MCP Server Integration

The /mcp command enables integration with Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, extending Claude's capabilities beyond file analysis.

Using MCP Servers

/mcp add <server-name> <server-config>
/mcp list
/mcp remove <server-name>

Example MCP Integration:

/mcp add local-database "postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb"
/mcp list

Once configured, Claude can use MCP server tools directly in conversations, providing access to external systems, databases, and specialized APIs.

Note: MCP server setup requires configuration outside of VS Code. Consult the specific MCP server documentation for initialization steps.

Plugin Management

The /plugins command provides access to Claude's plugin ecosystem, enabling enhanced functionality for specific workflows.

Plugin Commands

/plugins list
/plugins enable <plugin-name>
/plugins disable <plugin-name>
/plugins search <query>

Available Plugins:

  • Code analysis and optimization
  • Documentation generation
  • Test generation and validation
  • Security analysis
  • Performance profiling

Plugins extend Claude Code's capabilities for specialized development tasks. Browse available plugins using the search command.

Advanced Features

Chrome Integration

Claude Code version 1.0.36 and later includes integration with the Claude Chrome extension, enabling Claude to analyze web pages and web applications in context.

When both extensions are installed:

  • Claude can reference open web pages in conversations
  • Analyze frontend code rendered in the browser
  • Understand context from documentation websites
  • Debug web applications with visual feedback

Conversation History Management

VS Code automatically saves conversation history locally. To manage your history:

  1. Open the Claude panel
  2. Click the history icon (clock symbol)
  3. Browse previous conversations
  4. Click a conversation to resume it
  5. Archive or delete conversations as needed

Performance Optimization

For large projects, optimize Claude Code's performance:

  • Limit Context Size: Reference only necessary files and folders
  • Use Line Ranges: Specify exact line numbers to reduce token usage
  • Enable .gitignore: Let Claude respect your .gitignore to avoid unnecessary files
  • Archive Sessions: Close inactive sessions to reduce memory usage

JetBrains IDE Integration

Claude Code integrates seamlessly with JetBrains IDEs including IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, and other JetBrains products.

Installation

  1. Open JetBrains IDE
  2. Navigate to Preferences → Plugins (or IDE → Preferences → Plugins on macOS)
  3. Search for "Claude Code" in the Marketplace
  4. Click "Install"
  5. Restart the IDE when prompted
  6. Authenticate with your Claude account

Accessing Claude in JetBrains

  • Tool Window: Claude appears as a tool window on the right side of the IDE
  • Context Menu: Right-click files or code sections to reference them
  • Search: Use Ctrl+Shift+F (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Shift+F (macOS) to search and reference files

JetBrains-Specific Features

Run Configurations Claude can analyze and understand your run configurations, helping debug issues specific to your IDE setup.

Built-in Tools Reference JetBrains built-in tools and analyzers that Claude integrates with for comprehensive code understanding.

Git Integration Claude works with JetBrains' Git interface, understanding your branch structure and commit history.

Troubleshooting

Common Issues

Extension Not Showing

  • Verify VS Code version is 1.98.0 or later
  • Restart VS Code completely
  • Check Extension marketplace for installation status
  • Review Activity Bar for the Claude Code icon

Authentication Failures

  • Verify your Claude account credentials
  • Check internet connection
  • Sign out and sign in again via the extension settings
  • Clear VS Code cache if issues persist

File References Not Working

  • Ensure file paths are correct
  • Verify files exist in your project
  • Check that .gitignore isn't excluding necessary files
  • Try using the full file path instead of fuzzy matching

Performance Issues

  • Close unused conversation sessions
  • Limit the number of referenced files
  • Use line ranges for large files
  • Disable autosave for non-critical projects

Getting Help

  • Check the official Claude Code documentation
  • Review GitHub issues for known problems
  • Contact Anthropic support through your account dashboard
  • Consult IDE-specific forums for integration questions

Best Practices

Effective Claude Code Usage

  1. Clear Context: Provide specific file references rather than entire projects
  2. Focused Requests: Ask Claude to solve one problem at a time
  3. Review Changes: Always review Claude's proposed changes before accepting
  4. Plan Mode: Use "Plan" permission mode when trying new approaches
  5. Session Organization: Create separate sessions for distinct tasks
  6. Documentation: Reference API docs and specs when available

Security Considerations

  • Never share API keys in Claude conversations
  • Use permission modes carefully—review all changes before auto-accepting
  • Respect .gitignore and don't reference sensitive configuration files
  • Be cautious with Auto-Accept mode on shared machines
  • Regularly review conversation history for accidental information exposure

Summary

Claude Code's IDE integration brings powerful AI capabilities directly into your development workflow. By mastering file references, permission modes, keyboard shortcuts, and multi-session management, you can significantly enhance your productivity.

Whether you're using VS Code or JetBrains, Claude Code adapts to your preferred development environment, making AI-assisted development a seamless part of your daily work.

Start with Normal permission mode to understand Claude's capabilities, then gradually adopt more automated modes as you gain confidence. Use keyboard shortcuts to maintain flow, and leverage multi-session support to manage complex projects effectively.

Happy coding with Claude Code!

en.title: Claude Code Skills & Plugins — Extend AI Capabilities with SKILL.md
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how Claude Code and Cowork skills and plugins work. From writing SKILL.md files to creating plugins, using the skills.sh marketplace, and connecting external tools via MCP.
en.content:
What Are Skills?

Claude Code and Cowork come packed with many features out of the box, but by adding "skills," you can extend them to handle even more specialized tasks. A skill is a set of instructions written in a markdown file called SKILL.md that defines how Claude should behave in specific situations.

For example, you can create skills like "prioritize security and readability during code review" or "run tests before pushing to production during deployment" — custom instructions tailored to your team or personal workflow, saved and reusable across sessions.

Skills follow the Agent Skills open standard, making them compatible with AI tools beyond Claude Code.

Skill Structure

A skill is simply a directory containing a SKILL.md file.

my-skill/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instruction file (required)
├── reference.md       # Detailed reference (optional)
├── examples/
│   └── sample.md      # Output samples (optional)
└── scripts/
    └── helper.sh      # Executable scripts (optional)

The SKILL.md file consists of YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions.

Key Frontmatter Fields

The YAML frontmatter at the top of SKILL.md lets you fine-tune skill behavior.

name is the display name and becomes the /name slash command. description helps Claude decide whether to automatically invoke the skill based on context. Setting disable-model-invocation to true means the skill only runs when explicitly called with /name — ideal for workflows with side effects like deployments or commits.

allowed-tools specifies which tools can be used without permission during skill execution, and setting context to fork runs the skill as an isolated subagent.

Where to Save Skills

Where you place a skill determines its availability.

Personal skills go in ~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md and are available across all projects. Project skills go in .claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md and work only within that project. Plugin skills live in <plugin>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md and are available wherever the plugin is enabled.

Enterprise-wide distribution is possible through managed settings.

How to Invoke Skills

There are two ways to invoke a skill.

For manual invocation, use a slash command:

/code-review src/auth/login.ts

For automatic invocation, Claude evaluates the conversation context and loads the appropriate skill automatically. For example, if you ask "please review this code," a skill whose description mentions "code review" or "PR review" activates automatically.

You can pass arguments using the $ARGUMENTS placeholder:

Running /fix-issue 123 replaces $ARGUMENTS with 123.

Practical Examples

Example 1: Enforcing Commit Message Conventions

Example 2: PR Review with Dynamic Context

The !command`` syntax executes shell commands before the skill content is sent to Claude, injecting the results directly into the prompt.

What Are Plugins?

Plugins package skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers into a single distributable extension. If you only need skills for personal use, placing them in .claude/skills/ is sufficient, but plugins are the way to go when sharing with teams or distributing to the community.

The basic plugin structure looks like this:

my-plugin/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   └── plugin.json       # Plugin metadata
├── skills/
│   └── review/
│       └── SKILL.md
├── agents/
│   └── reviewer.md
├── hooks/
│   └── hooks.json
├── .mcp.json             # MCP server config
└── .lsp.json             # LSP server config

The plugin.json contains metadata like name, description, and version.

Installing and Managing Plugins

Install plugins from the CLI:

Control installation scope with --scope user (available across all projects, default), --scope project (shared with team via .claude/settings.json), or --scope local (project-local, gitignored).

Disabling, enabling, and uninstalling are also straightforward:

Plugin Marketplaces and skills.sh

Plugins are distributed through "marketplaces" — Git repositories or URL endpoints that catalog plugins.

The official marketplace is maintained by Anthropic and can be browsed from the Discover tab in Claude Code's /plugin menu. You can submit your own plugin at claude.ai/settings/plugins/submit.

skills.sh — The Community Skills Hub

skills.sh is a community hub where Claude Code skills and plugins are published. You can browse and search user-created skills, and install them easily through GitHub integration.

Over 100 production-ready skills covering engineering, marketing, compliance, and more are available. Simply register a marketplace and install what you need.

Team Marketplaces

For sharing proprietary plugins within your team, you can create a marketplace as a GitHub repository. Write a marketplace.json catalog, and team members can register the repository URL to install plugins from the /plugin menu.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) Integration

MCP is an open protocol for connecting AI tools to external data sources and services. Claude Code uses MCP to integrate with hundreds of services including Google Drive, Slack, Jira, GitHub, and databases.

Configuring MCP Servers

Add MCP servers using the claude mcp add command:

You can also use a .mcp.json configuration file for project-shared settings:

Environment variables like ${GITHUB_TOKEN} keep API keys out of the file, making it safe to share configurations across teams.

MCP Use Cases

With GitHub MCP configured, you can simply say "Fix Issue #42 and create a PR," and Claude will read the issue, implement the fix, and create a pull request automatically.

Figma MCP lets you pull component and style information directly from design files, enabling code generation based on actual designs. This dramatically streamlines the design-to-code workflow.

Google's Stitch MCP enables generating UI designs from text prompts and using them directly in Claude Code. You can browse projects, view screen designs, and extract design tokens — all from within your chat interface.

For mobile app development, Rork integration is also noteworthy. Rork Max uses Claude Code and Opus 4.6 as its backend to auto-generate native Swift apps from just an app description. Everything from UI to navigation, data persistence, and Apple API integration is handled by AI.

Slack MCP allows you to analyze messages in specific channels or automatically notify about Claude's work results.

Combining Plugins and MCP

Plugins can include .mcp.json files, allowing you to distribute skills and MCP server configurations as a package. For example, you could create a "Figma Design Review" plugin that bundles a Figma MCP server config with review skills, and distribute it to your entire team.

Skills in Cowork

Skills play an important role in the Cowork environment too. Cowork includes built-in skills for PDF processing, Excel creation, PowerPoint generation, and more — automatically loaded by Claude at the appropriate time.

For example, when you ask "create an Excel file," the xlsx skill activates automatically, handling library installation and formatting to produce high-quality spreadsheets.

You can add custom skills to Cowork as well. Create a .claude/skills/ directory in your working folder with SKILL.md files, and they'll be available in your Cowork sessions.

Bundled Skills

Claude Code comes with several pre-installed bundled skills.

/simplify reviews recently changed files for code reuse, quality, and efficiency, then applies improvements. It runs three review agents in parallel and aggregates results, making it far more efficient than manual review.

/batch orchestrates large-scale changes across the entire codebase in parallel. Provide a description of the change, and it analyzes the codebase, breaks it into tasks, and runs each in an independent git worktree. For example: /batch migrate src/ from Solid to React.

/debug analyzes Claude Code session debug logs to help troubleshoot issues.

Summary

Claude Code's skill and plugin system is a powerful toolkit for extending AI capabilities to match your needs. Writing a single SKILL.md file is all it takes to customize Claude's behavior.

Start with personal skills, share them with your team when they prove useful, and publish as plugins when they could benefit the wider community. If you find yourself giving Claude the same instructions repeatedly, that's a great candidate for turning into a skill.

en.title: Claude for Education — AI in the Classroom
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Explore Claude for Education and how it transforms teaching and learning. From student learning support to teacher workflow optimization, discover education-specific features and practical use cases.
en.content:
Claude for Education — AI in the Classroom

Claude for Education is a specialized Claude plan designed for educational institutions. It provides features that support student learning while streamlining teacher workflows. This guide covers effective ways to use Claude in educational settings.

What is Claude for Education?
