Claude for Education is a program that enables universities and educational institutions to safely adopt Claude. Key features include:

  • Learning support mode: A Socratic dialogue approach that encourages students to think critically rather than providing direct answers
  • Institutional management: Administrators can centrally manage user access and policies
  • Academic accuracy: Response style that emphasizes citations and references
  • Privacy protection: Designed with student data protection in mind

For Students

1. Deepening Understanding

Claude takes a "guide understanding" approach rather than "giving answers":

Effective question example:

Explain the separation of variables method for differential equations
intuitively and step by step. Include "why we do this" at each step.

Claude generates responses that build understanding progressively from foundational concepts. You can deepen comprehension by asking follow-up questions on parts you don't understand.

2. Report and Essay Feedback

Have Claude review your drafts and provide improvement feedback:

Please give specific feedback on the structure and logical flow
of the following report. Don't rewrite the text — just provide
advice for improvement.

This approach preserves students' opportunity to improve their work independently.

3. Language Learning Support

Claude supports multiple languages, making it a powerful language learning partner:

  • Grammar explanations with example sentences
  • Writing corrections and feedback
  • Conversation practice simulations
  • Cultural nuance explanations

4. Programming Education

When learning to code, Claude provides step-by-step hints:

I want to implement bubble sort in Python, but instead of
the complete code, please give me the algorithmic thinking
and hints for each step.

For Teachers

1. Course Material Creation

Claude streamlines the creation of lesson plans and slide outlines:

  • Drafting syllabi
  • Generating lecture slide outlines
  • Creating practice problems and quizzes
  • Curriculum design based on learning objectives

2. Assignment Design

Support for designing effective assignments:

Design a data structures assignment for second-year university students.
Include progressive problems from knowledge recall to application,
based on Bloom's taxonomy.

3. Rubric Creation

Create rubrics that clarify evaluation criteria:

Create a rubric for evaluating undergraduate research reports.
Include 5 evaluation axes: paper structure, evidence quality,
critical thinking, writing quality, and citation accuracy,
with 4 levels for each axis.

4. Research Support

Claude also supports teachers' own research activities:

  • Organizing literature reviews
  • Discussing research methodologies
  • Exploring data analysis approaches
  • Providing feedback on paper drafts

Institutional Deployment

Administration Features

Claude for Education allows institutional administrators to control:

  • User management
  • Usage policy configuration
  • Usage monitoring
  • Access permission management

Academic Integrity Considerations

Claude for Education prioritizes academic integrity:

  • Response design that respects students' opportunity to think independently
  • Provides feedback rather than ghostwriting reports or essays
  • Compliance with institutional usage policies
  • Emphasis on the importance of citations and sources

Deployment Steps

  1. Needs assessment: Clarify how the institution wants to leverage AI
  2. Policy development: Create AI usage guidelines for students and faculty
  3. Pilot deployment: Trial in selected courses
  4. Training: Conduct workshops for faculty and students
  5. Full rollout: Scale institution-wide based on feedback

Effective Prompting Tips for Education

Student Prompt Templates

Understanding concepts:

Explain [topic] step by step, assuming no prior knowledge.
Include comprehension check questions at each step.

Building problem-solving skills:

Give me hints to solve the following problem in 3 stages.
First, just the direction; then a specific approach;
finally, the first step of the solution.
Don't give me the answer itself.

Teacher Prompt Templates

Lesson planning:

Create a 15-session lesson plan for [subject].
Include each session's theme, learning objectives,
main activities, and pre-class assignments.

Practice problem generation:

Create 10 practice problems about [topic].
Divide difficulty into basic (4), applied (4),
and advanced (2), with answer guides for each.

Privacy and Security

Claude for Education is designed with educational data protection in mind:

  • Student personal information is protected
  • Conversation data is not used for model training (Business plan and above)
  • Compatible with institutional data policies
  • SOC 2 Type II certified

Summary

Claude for Education is a solution for safely and effectively bringing AI into education. It supports student-driven learning while reducing teacher workload. The key is using AI not as a "tool that gives answers" but as a "partner that deepens learning." We recommend a gradual deployment approach aligned with your institution's policies.

en.title: Claude Vision (Image Recognition) Guide
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude's image recognition capabilities. Covers input methods, supported formats, token calculation, and best practices.
en.content:
Introduction

Claude's Vision capability is a powerful multimodal feature that processes images alongside text. Whether you're analyzing photos, interpreting charts, understanding screenshots, or extracting information from documents, Claude can handle it all. This comprehensive guide covers everything you need to know about leveraging Claude's image recognition capabilities effectively, from basic setup to advanced optimization strategies.

Supported Formats and Specifications

Image Format Support

Claude Vision supports four primary image formats, each with specific advantages:

  • JPEG - The most widely used format with excellent compression and compatibility
  • PNG - Lossless compression that preserves image quality and supports transparency
  • GIF - Animated image support (processes the first frame)
  • WebP - Modern format offering superior compression efficiency

Multiple Image Processing

Claude's Vision API supports processing up to 100 images in a single request. This capability enables:

  • Complex comparative analysis across multiple images
  • Batch processing of related documents
  • Enhanced context understanding through multiple perspectives
  • Efficient workflow automation for document processing

Tip: When approaching the 100-image limit, consider grouping related images and submitting multiple requests to manage processing load and improve response quality.

Three Methods for Image Input

Method 1: Base64 Encoding

Convert your image to base64 format and embed it directly in your API request. This method works well for testing, smaller images, and scenarios where you want to include binary data directly in requests.

Advantages:

  • No external URLs required
  • Works offline or with private images
  • Useful for file processing pipelines

Disadvantages:

  • Larger request payloads
  • Requires file reading and encoding overhead

Method 2: URL Reference

Reference publicly accessible images directly via URL. This approach is fast, efficient, and eliminates the need to upload file contents.

Advantages:

  • Smallest request payload
  • Fast processing
  • Ideal for web-based images

Disadvantages:

  • Image must be publicly accessible
  • URL dependency and potential availability issues
  • Privacy concerns with sensitive content

Important: Images referenced via URL must be publicly accessible. Private or authentication-protected images require Base64 or Files API methods.

Method 3: Files API

For production environments, large files, and scenarios requiring long-term storage and reference, the Files API provides robust file management.

Advantages:

  • Secure file storage
  • File reusability without re-uploading
  • Production-grade reliability
  • Best for sensitive or large files

Disadvantages:

  • Requires file upload step
  • Files stored with associated costs
  • Additional API calls required

Token Calculation and Cost Optimization

Claude's Vision implementation uses a token-based pricing model where images consume tokens based on their dimensions. Understanding this calculation is crucial for cost optimization.

Token Calculation Formula

The fundamental token calculation for images follows this formula:

Tokens = (image_width × image_height) ÷ 750

This formula applies to all images regardless of format. Higher resolution images consume more tokens, directly impacting API costs.

Token Examples by Image Size

| Image Dimensions | Calculation | Approximate Tokens | |------------------|-------------|-------------------| | 200×200px | (200 × 200) ÷ 750 | ~53 tokens | | 400×400px | (400 × 400) ÷ 750 | ~213 tokens | | 600×600px | (600 × 600) ÷ 750 | ~480 tokens | | 800×800px | (800 × 800) ÷ 750 | ~853 tokens | | 1000×1000px | (1000 × 1000) ÷ 750 | ~1,333 tokens | | 1568×1568px | (1568 × 1568) ÷ 750 | ~3,275 tokens |

Pricing Breakdown

Image processing costs vary based on resolution. Here's the current pricing structure:

Small Image (200×200px)

  • Token Count: ~53 tokens
  • Estimated Cost: ~$0.00016

Medium Image (600×600px)

  • Token Count: ~480 tokens
  • Estimated Cost: ~$0.00145

Large Image (1092×1092px)

  • Token Count: ~1,588 tokens
  • Estimated Cost: ~$0.0048

Cost Calculation Utility

Cost Optimization Strategies

  1. Resize Before Upload: Resize images to 1568px or smaller before sending
  2. Batch Processing: Group multiple images in single requests when possible
  3. Format Selection: Choose efficient formats like WebP for better compression
  4. Selective Analysis: Send only relevant image portions rather than full images

Best Practices for Optimal Results

1. Strategic Image Placement

Always place images before text prompts. This ordering allows Claude to properly contextualize the image content before interpreting your questions, resulting in more accurate and thoughtful responses.

2. Image Resizing for Performance

Optimize images by resizing them to a maximum of 1568px on their longest dimension. This reduces token consumption and accelerates processing without sacrificing quality.

3. Effective Use of Multiple Images

When analyzing multiple images, group them logically and provide clear comparative context.

4. Detailed and Specific Instructions

Precision in your prompts directly correlates with accuracy in responses. Provide explicit instructions about what you want Claude to focus on and how to structure the response.

Capabilities and Limitations

Understanding both the strengths and boundaries of Claude Vision ensures you use it appropriately and achieve reliable results.

1. No Face Identification

Claude cannot identify, recognize, or provide information about specific individuals in images. This is a fundamental limitation implemented for privacy and safety reasons.

2. Spatial Reasoning Limitations

Claude's ability to understand precise spatial relationships, especially in complex 3D environments, has limitations. Approximate spatial descriptions work better than exact measurements.

3. Approximate Counting Only

Object counting, especially in dense crowds or complex scenes, yields approximate rather than exact numbers. Small, clearly distinct objects count more accurately than large groups.

Warning: Never rely on Claude's image counting for financial, medical, or critical business decisions. Always implement human verification for any count-dependent processes.

Practical Use Case Examples

Use Case 1: Document and Receipt Processing

Automatically extract and structure information from scanned documents, receipts, and invoices.

Use Case 2: Chart and Data Visualization Analysis

Extract insights from business intelligence dashboards, reports, and statistical visualizations.

Use Case 3: Website and UI Analysis

Evaluate user interfaces, assess design quality, and identify usability issues.

Troubleshooting Guide

Images Not Processing Correctly

Symptom: API returns errors or refuses to process image

Solutions:

  1. Verify file format is JPEG, PNG, GIF, or WebP
  2. Ensure image is not corrupted (try opening in image viewer)
  3. For Base64: Verify proper encoding with len(image_data) > 0
  4. For URLs: Test accessibility in browser
  5. Check file size isn't excessively large

Low Analysis Quality

Symptom: Responses are vague or inaccurate

Solutions:

  1. Increase image resolution (up to 1568px recommended)
  2. Provide more specific, detailed prompts
  3. Add context and background information
  4. Ensure sufficient contrast and clarity
  5. Try repositioning image before text

Rate Limiting

Symptom: "Rate limit exceeded" errors

Solutions:

  1. Implement exponential backoff retry logic
  2. Reduce request frequency
  3. Batch images more efficiently
  4. Check API quota usage

Unexpected API Errors

Symptom: 400, 401, 500 errors

Solutions:

  1. Verify API key is valid and active
  2. Check model identifier spelling
  3. Ensure request payload is valid JSON
  4. Review error message for specific issues
  5. Check API status page for outages

Summary and Key Takeaways

Claude Vision enables powerful multimodal analysis by combining visual understanding with advanced language capabilities. Success depends on understanding both capabilities and limitations.

Key Principles:

  • Always place images before text prompts for optimal context
  • Resize images to 1568px or smaller for efficiency and cost savings
  • Use the three input methods (Base64, URL, Files API) appropriately for your use case
  • Calculate token costs using the formula: (width × height) ÷ 750
  • Provide detailed, specific instructions for best results
  • Remember limitations: no face identification, approximate spatial reasoning, estimated counts only

Next Steps:

  • Start with simple use cases to understand capabilities
  • Experiment with different prompt structures
  • Monitor token usage and costs
  • Gradually increase complexity as you gain experience
  • Build robust error handling for production systems

By following these guidelines and best practices, you can build reliable, efficient applications that leverage Claude's vision capabilities to their fullest potential.

For deeper study of LLMs and multimodal AI, see our recommended AI & LLM books for all skill levels.

en.title: Complete Guide to Tool Use — Calling External Tools with the Claude API
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Master tool use (function calling) with the Claude API. Learn to define tools, handle results, and integrate external services seamlessly.
en.content:
What is Tool Use?

Tool use, also known as function calling, allows Claude to request the execution of external tools and functions. Rather than generating text about what it would do, Claude can directly invoke APIs, call functions, and interact with external systems.

ℹ️
Tool use enables Claude to: - Query databases and APIs - Read and write files - Perform calculations - Execute custom business logic - Integrate with external services

Basic Tool Definition

Tools are defined in your API request using the tools parameter. Each tool has a name, description, and input schema.

Tool Schema Structure

Tool Definition Best Practices

  • Clear names: Use descriptive tool names (e.g., query_customer_db not func1)
  • Detailed descriptions: Explain what the tool does and when to use it
  • Complete schema: Define all parameters and their constraints
  • Specify required fields: Mark parameters that must be provided
  • Add examples: Include enum values or constraints in descriptions

Python Implementation

Define Tools

Make a Request with Tools

Handling Tool Results

When Claude decides to use a tool, it returns a tool_use content block with the tool name and input parameters. You must then execute the tool and return the result.

Processing Tool Use Responses

Implementing Tool Handlers

Multi-Turn Conversations with Tool Use

Tool use typically requires multiple turns: Claude requests a tool, you execute it, and Claude continues based on the result.

Complete Multi-Turn Example

The tool_choice Parameter

The tool_choice parameter controls when Claude can use tools.

Tool Choice Options

TypeScript Implementation

Define and Use Tools

Error Handling

Always handle tool execution errors gracefully:

When returning errors to Claude, always include them in the tool result:

Best Practices

  1. Design clear tool boundaries: Each tool should do one thing well
  2. Validate inputs: Check parameters before executing
  3. Return structured results: Return JSON with clear success/error fields
  4. Provide context: Give Claude clear error messages to work with
  5. Limit tool count: 3-5 well-designed tools work better than 20 mediocre ones
  6. Test thoroughly: Test all tool paths before production
  7. Document parameters: Make tool descriptions comprehensive
  8. Handle timeouts: Set reasonable execution timeouts for tools

Common Patterns

Database Queries

API Calls

Next Steps

  • Implement your first tool for a real-world use case
  • Build a multi-turn conversation with tool use
  • Create tools for your application's core functionality
  • Monitor tool execution and error rates in production

en.title: Cowork × Excel / PowerPoint Integration — Automate Office Tasks with AI
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to create, edit, and analyze Excel spreadsheets and PowerPoint presentations using Claude's Cowork mode. From data analysis to slide generation, discover practical techniques for automating office workflows.
en.content:
Cowork × Excel / PowerPoint Integration

Cowork mode allows Claude to directly manipulate files in a local environment, making it possible to create and analyze Excel spreadsheets and auto-generate PowerPoint presentations. This guide covers practical use cases and techniques.

Cowork's Office File Support

Cowork runs on a lightweight Linux VM and leverages Python libraries to directly manipulate Office files:

  • openpyxl: Reading and writing Excel (.xlsx) files
  • python-pptx: Creating and editing PowerPoint (.pptx) files
  • pandas: Data analysis with Excel integration

Excel Integration in Practice

Creating Spreadsheets

Simply ask Cowork to "create a sales report Excel" and it generates a formatted spreadsheet.

Example request:

Create an Excel report from the monthly sales data.
Apply colored formatting to headers, add a totals row,
and include a month-over-month comparison column.

Cowork automatically handles:

  • Structuring data and placing it in cells
  • Header formatting (fonts, background colors, borders)
  • Inserting formulas (SUM, percentage changes, etc.)
  • Auto-adjusting column widths
  • Applying conditional formatting

Data Analysis and Visualization

Pass an existing Excel file to Cowork for analysis:

Analyze the sales data in this Excel file and add
a monthly trend chart and a top 10 products ranking table.

What Cowork can analyze and visualize:

  • Creating pivot tables
  • Inserting line charts, bar charts, and pie charts
  • Calculating statistics (mean, median, standard deviation)
  • Sorting and filtering data
  • Highlighting with conditional formatting

CSV Conversion

Converting CSV files to formatted Excel is straightforward:

Convert this CSV file to Excel.
Format it as a table and apply comma-separated
number formatting to numeric columns.

PowerPoint Integration in Practice

Automated Presentation Generation

Cowork can create presentations from scratch based on a topic:

Create a PowerPoint for "Q1 2026 Business Report."
Structure it in 5 sections: title slide, table of contents,
performance summary, department results, and future plans.

Generated slides include:

  • Title slides and section dividers
  • Auto-arranged text content
  • Embedded tables and charts
  • Consistent design themes
  • Speaker notes

Editing Existing Slides

You can also edit existing PowerPoint files:

Update the chart data on page 3 of this PowerPoint.
Also, add a summary slide at the end.

Supported edit operations:

  • Modifying and adding text
  • Adding, removing, and reordering slides
  • Inserting and replacing images
  • Updating tables
  • Adjusting design elements

Generating Slides Directly from Data

Create chart-filled slides directly from Excel data or CSVs:

Create a management meeting presentation from this sales Excel.
Include a KPI dashboard slide and detailed department slides.

Practical Workflow Examples

Example 1: Monthly Report Automation

  1. Ask "Create a monthly report Excel from last month's sales CSV"
  2. Cowork reads the data and generates a formatted Excel file
  3. Charts and summaries are added automatically
  4. Download the completed file

Example 2: Meeting Material Preparation

  1. Tell Cowork your agenda and key points
  2. PowerPoint slides are auto-generated
  3. Give refinement instructions like "add a chart to page 3"
  4. Download the finished presentation

Example 3: Data Aggregation and Reporting

  1. Pass multiple Excel files to Cowork
  2. Ask "aggregate these data files and create a summary report"
  3. A consolidated Excel and presentation are generated

Claude in Excel Add-in

Separately from Cowork, the Claude in Excel add-in lets you use Claude directly within Excel for:

  • In-cell data analysis and summarization
  • Pivot table editing
  • Conditional formatting setup
  • Formula suggestions and corrections

Claude in Excel uses the Opus 4.6 model and supports native Excel operations.

Tips and Best Practices

Effective Request Tips

  • Be specific: Instead of "make it nice," specify "blue header with white text, right-align numbers"
  • Describe the end goal: Explain who will see the file and what it should communicate
  • Build incrementally: For complex files, make incremental requests rather than one large ask
  • Select your folder: Pre-select your working folder for smooth file reading and writing

Considerations

  • Large files (tens of thousands of rows) may take longer to process
  • Macro-enabled .xlsm files have limited macro editing support
  • PowerPoint animation settings have limited support
  • Fonts depend on the environment; use caution with specialty fonts

Summary

Leveraging Cowork's Excel and PowerPoint integration dramatically streamlines routine office tasks. From CSV and data analysis to formatted report generation and automated presentation creation, Cowork lets you accomplish it all with natural language instructions. Start small and integrate it into your daily workflow.

en.title: Cowork 2026 Updates — Windows Support, Scheduled Tasks & New Connectors
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: A roundup of Cowork's latest features in 2026: Windows support, scheduled tasks, plugin marketplace, new MCP connectors, and more.
en.content:
Cowork 2026 Updates — A Complete Overview

2026 has been a transformative year for Cowork. With full Windows support, powerful scheduled tasks, an expanding plugin marketplace, and an impressive array of MCP connectors, Cowork has evolved into an indispensable tool for both individual users and enterprises. This article covers all the major features and improvements you need to know about.

What is Cowork?

Cowork is a revolutionary desktop application that brings the power of Claude Code to everyday users. Rather than restricting advanced AI assistance to developers, Cowork democratizes intelligent automation—enabling everyone to leverage AI for daily tasks like file organization, data analysis, document creation, and workflow automation.

Whether you're managing emails, generating reports, analyzing spreadsheets, or building complex multi-step workflows, Cowork transforms time-consuming work into efficient, AI-assisted processes.

ℹ️
Cowork launched on macOS in 2025 and has now expanded to full Windows support in 2026, providing equal functionality across both platforms.

Windows Support — Feature Parity with macOS

The most significant milestone of 2026 is complete Windows support. What was previously exclusive to macOS is now available to Windows users with identical functionality.

Complete Feature Parity

Cowork for Windows delivers the exact same feature set as its macOS counterpart:

  • File Access — Seamless interaction with your local file system, folder monitoring, and directory operations
  • Multi-Step Tasks — Automation of complex workflows with ordered execution of multiple steps
  • Plugin Support — Installation and execution of third-party plugins to extend functionality
  • MCP Connectors — Full integration with Google Drive, Gmail, Slack, and dozens of other services

Windows users now enjoy the same powerful experience as macOS users, enabling smooth collaboration across teams regardless of operating system.

💡
If you use both Windows and macOS, you can sync your settings and plugins across devices. Start work on one machine and continue seamlessly on another with the same configuration.

Scheduled Tasks — Automation at Scale

One of the most powerful additions to Cowork in 2026 is scheduled tasks. What once required manual execution can now run automatically on a regular schedule.

Flexible Scheduling with Cron Expressions

Scheduled tasks support standard cron syntax for precise control over when tasks run:

0 9 * * *        # Every day at 9:00 AM
0 9 * * 1-5      # Weekdays (Mon-Fri) at 9:00 AM
30 8 * * 1       # Every Monday at 8:30 AM
0 0 1 * *        # First day of each month at midnight

On-Demand Execution

Beyond scheduled runs, you can manually trigger any task at any time. Need to run something right now? Execute it immediately without waiting for the next scheduled window.

Stored as SKILL.md Files

All tasks are saved as SKILL.md Markdown files, making task definitions text-based and easily manageable. This approach enables:

  • Version control integration
  • Simple sharing with team members
  • Easy editing in any text editor
  • Clear documentation of task logic
# Daily Sales Report

Schedule: 0 9 * * *
Timezone: America/New_York

This task generates yesterday's sales report
and emails it to the leadership team with
key metrics and insights.
ℹ️
Because SKILL.md files are plain text, you can manage complex task definitions using your favorite editor, and track changes using Git or other version control systems.

Plugins & Marketplace

Extending Cowork's capabilities has become easier than ever with the plugin marketplace.

Install from skills.sh

The skills.sh platform serves as a central hub for discovering and installing plugins. The process is simple: search, review, and install with a single click.

Example plugins now available:

  • Data Analysis — Automated processing of Excel and CSV files
  • Email Management — Intelligent inbox organization and filtering
  • Document Generation — Template-based document creation and formatting
  • Translation — Multi-language support and content localization
  • Social Media — Content scheduling and analytics across platforms

Private Enterprise Marketplace

For organizations, Cowork offers a private marketplace where companies can distribute internally-developed and approved plugins. This ensures:

  • Compliance with security policies
  • Standardization across the organization
  • Control over which tools team members can access
  • Streamlined plugin discovery for enterprise users

New MCP Connectors — Integrate with Everything

The expansion of MCP (Model Context Protocol) connectors in 2026 is remarkable. Cowork can now connect directly to dozens of popular services and platforms.

Available MCP Connectors

Cloud Storage & Documents:

  • Google Drive
  • Google Docs
  • Google Sheets
  • Dropbox

Email & Calendar:

  • Gmail
  • Google Calendar
  • Outlook
  • Microsoft Exchange

Productivity & Collaboration:

  • Slack
  • Microsoft Teams
  • Notion
  • Asana
  • Monday.com

Sales & Marketing:

  • Apollo (Sales Intelligence)
  • Clay (Data Automation)
  • HubSpot
  • Salesforce

Document & Signature Management:

  • DocuSign (E-Signatures)
  • Dropbox Sign

Web & Content:

  • WordPress (Website Management)
  • Medium

Finance & Data:

  • FactSet (Financial Data)
  • Bloomberg Terminal
  • Stripe (Payments)
💡
New connectors are added monthly based on user demand. Check the skills.sh connector directory regularly to discover the latest integrations.

Real-World Connector Use Cases

Combining connectors enables sophisticated workflows:

Use Case 1: Automated Email Management & Weekly Reports

  1. Fetch emails from Gmail for the past week
  2. Categorize by keyword and sender
  3. Aggregate results in Google Sheets
  4. Send summary to team via Slack
  5. Run automatically every Monday at 8 AM

Use Case 2: Sales Pipeline Automation

  1. Pull prospect data from Apollo
  2. Enrich with company info from Clay
  3. Update Salesforce CRM automatically
  4. Notify assigned salespeople via Slack
  5. Log all activity for compliance

Use Case 3: Content Publishing Workflow

  1. Write content in Google Docs
  2. Convert to WordPress-ready format
  3. Publish to WordPress site automatically
  4. Share announcement on Slack
  5. Post to social media (coming soon)

Global Folder Instructions — Customize Your Workspace

New in 2026: Global Folder Instructions let you define how AI should behave in specific directories.

Folder-Specific Guidelines

Create a .claude-instructions file in any folder to provide context-specific guidance:

# .claude-instructions

This folder contains client presentations.

When working with files here:
- Follow corporate brand guidelines
- Use only Arial or Helvetica fonts
- Stick to brand colors (RGB: 0, 102, 204)
- Include company logo on every slide
- Add legal disclaimer footer
- Proofread for accuracy before finalizing

Benefits

  • Consistency — Automatic adherence to style guides and standards
  • Efficiency — No need to repeat instructions for every task
  • Team Alignment — Ensures uniform quality and branding across projects
  • Knowledge Preservation — Guidelines are documented and shared

App Context Sharing — Seamless Office Integration

Cowork in 2026 integrates deeply with Microsoft Office, creating an unprecedented level of app synergy.

Context Flow Between Cowork, Excel & PowerPoint

  • Cowork → Excel — Push AI-generated data and analysis directly into spreadsheets
  • Excel → Cowork — Import charts, tables, and raw data for further analysis
  • Cowork → PowerPoint — Auto-format generated content as presentation slides
  • Cross-App Workflows — Chain operations across applications effortlessly

Practical Example: Monthly Executive Report

A sales manager needs to create a monthly report in three steps:

  1. In Cowork: Request "Analyze this month's sales by region and product category"
  2. AI generates analysis and automatically inserts results into Excel
  3. Excel creates charts and visualizations from the data
  4. PowerPoint auto-imports the charts and formats them into slides with branding
  5. Manager reviews and sends to executives—no manual copying required

The entire workflow is interconnected, eliminating repetitive data transfer and formatting.

ℹ️
This app synergy significantly accelerates document creation. It's especially powerful for data-heavy reports, quarterly business reviews, and presentation decks that require analysis and visualization.

Enterprise Management Features

To support large-scale organizational deployments, Cowork offers comprehensive enterprise management capabilities.

Private Plugin Marketplace

Organizations can establish their own plugin marketplace, distributing custom plugins exclusively to their users.

Advantages:

  • Enforce security and compliance requirements
  • Centralize license management
  • Promote organizational standards
  • Control plugin visibility and distribution

Access Control & User Management

  • Plugin/Connector Restrictions by user level or department
  • Group-Based Permissions for granular access control
  • Complete Audit Logging of all actions and access
  • Single Sign-On (SSO) with Active Directory, Okta, and other providers

Data Governance & Security

  • Encryption of data at rest and in transit
  • Regional Data Residency — data stored only in specified geographic locations
  • Compliance Certifications — GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2 Type II
  • Data Retention Policies — automated deletion based on defined rules
  • Encrypted Backup & Recovery — protect against data loss
💡
Enterprise plans include dedicated support teams to assist with deployment, security hardening, customization, and staff training. Your organization's unique needs are fully supported.

Performance Improvements & Stability

Beyond new features, 2026 brought significant improvements to speed and reliability.

Speed Enhancements

  • 30% faster average AI response times through optimization
  • Quicker file operations with improved I/O handling
  • Instant plugin loading with better caching strategies
  • Reduced startup time on both Windows and macOS

Stability & Reliability

  • Fixed memory leak issues that affected long-running tasks
  • Enhanced crash recovery with automatic session restoration
  • Improved error handling with better error messages
  • New diagnostic tools for troubleshooting

Roadmap: What's Coming Next

The Cowork team is actively developing exciting features for future releases:

  • Autonomous Agents — AI that can handle even more complex, multi-day tasks with minimal supervision
  • Real-Time Collaboration — Live co-editing and simultaneous work by multiple team members
  • Mobile App — Bring Cowork to iOS and Android for on-the-go automation
  • Advanced Analytics — Dashboard for task performance, automation metrics, and ROI
  • Voice Interaction — Natural voice commands to trigger tasks and queries
  • Enterprise Systems — Connectors for SAP, Oracle, Workday, and other major platforms
  • Blockchain Integration — Smart contract interaction and Web3 support

Conclusion

Cowork's 2026 release represents a major leap forward in AI-assisted productivity. Three pillars define this update:

  1. Windows Support — Equal functionality regardless of operating system
  2. Scheduled Tasks — Automation of repetitive work on any schedule
  3. MCP Connectors — Deep integration with 40+ popular services

Whether you're a solopreneur looking to automate daily tasks or an enterprise managing thousands of employees, Cowork provides the tools to work smarter, faster, and more strategically.

Get Started with Cowork

  • Official Site: https://cowork.claude.com
  • Documentation: https://docs.cowork.claude.com
  • Community Forum: https://community.cowork.claude.com
  • Download: https://cowork.claude.com/download

en.title: Cowork Connectors Guide — Maximize AI Power with External Service Integration
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Cowork's connector features. From integrating with Canva, Figma, Slack, and Asana to interactive tool integration via MCP Apps, discover practical ways to connect external services.
en.content:
What Are Connectors?

Connectors are the bridges between Cowork and your favorite external services. Built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP) standard, they enable Claude to interact directly with tools like Canva, Figma, Slack, and Asana without requiring manual context switching. Instead of copying and pasting information between applications, you can leverage Claude's intelligence to work seamlessly across your entire software ecosystem.

Think of connectors as specialized agents that understand each service's unique language and capabilities. When you request an action—whether it's creating a design, analyzing data, or managing tasks—the connector translates your intent into the appropriate API calls and returns results directly into your conversation.

Your Connected Service Toolkit

Cowork currently supports integrations with several major platforms, each opening different possibilities for automation and analysis.

Canva stands out as a creative powerhouse. You can search through your design library, edit existing presentations and documents, and export polished materials in multiple formats. One of the most practical applications is generating presentations from templates and immediately exporting them as PDFs. Instead of manually building slides one by one, you can describe your presentation topic, let Claude generate a structured outline, create the slides from a Canva template, and export the final product—all within one conversation.

Figma brings your design systems into the AI workflow. Extract components, inspect styling information, and understand your design library's structure. This becomes incredibly powerful when combined with code generation. Imagine describing a React component you need built, having Claude analyze your Figma design file to understand the exact colors, typography, and spacing, then generating code that matches your design system perfectly.

Slack transforms team communication into actionable intelligence. Analyze channel messages to understand discussions, sentiment, and decisions. Generate reports summarizing key points from threads. Create notifications that alert your team when specific conditions are met. This connector lets you turn chat histories into insights without manual review.

Asana integrates task management into your workflow. View project status, track task progress, and understand team workload. Claude can help you identify bottlenecks, suggest reallocation of resources, and keep everyone synchronized on project status without requiring constant manual status checks.

Google Drive and Box provide document access and file management capabilities. Retrieve necessary context from your stored documents, manage files, and integrate document data into larger workflows. This is particularly useful when you need to pull information from multiple sources to create comprehensive reports or analyses.

GitHub enables intelligent repository management. Create issues, manage pull requests, review code changes, and automate common development workflows. Claude can analyze your codebase issues, suggest PRs for review, and help coordinate development activities.

Connecting Your Services

Getting started is refreshingly simple. Within the Cowork interface, you'll find a connection panel where available services are displayed. Click on any service you want to integrate, and Cowork will guide you through a streamlined OAuth authentication flow. This means you're granting Cowork secure, permission-specific access to your account—not sharing passwords or credentials.

Once connected, the service appears as available within Claude's context. You don't need to manually select or configure anything for each request. Your connected services are simply there, ready to be used when relevant.

MCP Apps: Direct Tool Integration

As of January 2026, Cowork introduced MCP Apps, a significant evolution in how Claude interacts with external services. Rather than working through traditional connectors alone, MCP Apps allow you to operate tools directly within Claude's chat interface. Tools become native capabilities—you can use them exactly as you would use any built-in Claude function, with full visibility into what's happening and direct control over each action.

This means you're not just connecting to services; you're bringing their power directly into your conversation. When you need to search for a Canva design or update an Asana task, the interaction feels as native as any other Claude capability.

Real-World Examples

Creating and Exporting a Presentation

You're preparing for a quarterly business review. Instead of opening Canva, creating slides manually, and exporting the file, you simply tell Claude: "Generate a presentation about Q1 results with our company's template, including sections on revenue growth, customer acquisition, and product roadmap, then export it as PDF."

Claude uses the Canva connector to find your company template, generates a structured outline, creates the slides with your branding, and exports the final PDF—all in one request. You get a polished presentation ready for your meeting.

Analyzing Team Communication

You want to understand what your engineering team discussed in Slack this month. Ask Claude to analyze messages from your #engineering channel, summarize key decisions, identify blockers, and flag action items. The Slack connector retrieves the conversation history, Claude processes it, and you receive a clear summary highlighting important patterns without reading through hundreds of messages manually.

Building Components from Design

Your design team created mockups in Figma for a new dashboard. Rather than manually translating those designs to code, describe to Claude what you need: "Create a React component for the dashboard widget shown in our Figma design file, matching the exact colors, spacing, and typography."

Claude accesses your Figma file, extracts the styling information and component structure, and generates React code that matches your design system. The result is code that's visually accurate and maintains consistency with your broader design system.

When to Use Claude in Chrome

While connectors handle direct service integration beautifully, some situations call for browser automation via Claude in Chrome. When no connector exists for a service you need, or when a task requires human-like browser interaction that traditional APIs can't support, Claude in Chrome becomes your fallback. This browser automation capability lets you interact with almost any web application directly.

Use Claude in Chrome when you're working with niche tools, legacy systems, or when the interaction you need goes beyond what a connector provides. It's more flexible but slightly less streamlined than connectors—a worthwhile tradeoff when no direct integration exists.

Getting the Most from Connectors

The real power of connectors emerges when you combine them strategically. Pull data from Slack to identify team blockers, create a presentation analyzing those patterns in Canva, add relevant code snippets from your GitHub repository, and share the complete analysis with your team through Asana. What would take hours of context switching now flows in one conversation.

Start by connecting the services you use most frequently. Then experiment with requests that span multiple tools. As you discover workflows that work well, you'll find connectors becoming an invisible part of how you work—a multiplier on your productivity that makes AI assistance feel like a natural extension of your toolkit.

Connectors represent a shift in how we think about AI assistance: not as isolated tools, but as intelligent coordinators capable of orchestrating work across your entire digital ecosystem. When your AI knows how to access the services and data you actually use, it becomes infinitely more useful.

en.title: Cursor × Claude Code Integration Guide — The Ultimate AI Dev Combo
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: How to combine Cursor editor with Claude Code CLI for maximum development efficiency. Setup, practical workflows, and tips for using both tools together.
en.content:
Cursor × Claude Code Integration Guide

Modern AI-native development combines the strengths of specialized tools. Cursor editor and Claude Code CLI each bring unique capabilities to the table. When used together strategically, they create a development experience that transcends what either tool could achieve alone. This guide explores how to leverage both tools effectively and establish workflows that maximize productivity.

What is Cursor?

Cursor is an AI-native code editor built on VS Code. It's not simply VS Code with an AI plugin—AI is baked into the core of the editor itself.

Key Features:

  • AI-powered code completion — Contextual predictions as you type
  • Chat within code — Ask questions and get answers without leaving the editor
  • Command mode — Use Cmd+K to instruct AI to modify code
  • VS Code compatibility — All extensions and keybindings work seamlessly
  • Claude Sonnet built-in — The powerful Claude 3.5 Sonnet is the default model
  • Per-file context — AI understands the current file deeply and instantly

Cursor excels at localized, focused editing: UI refinement, bug fixes, logic adjustments, and interactive coding sessions. It's designed for sprints of concentrated work on specific components or functions.

Why Combine Claude Code with Cursor?

While Cursor is powerful on its own, adding Claude Code CLI extends your toolkit to handle larger project scopes and architectural changes.

Claude Code's Strengths:

  • Grasps entire project context at once
  • Makes coordinated changes across many files
  • Creates, runs, and interprets test suites
  • Handles Git operations and PR workflows
  • Discovers patterns and opportunities across the codebase
  • Generates complete feature scaffolds from scratch

Cursor's Strengths:

  • Rapid iteration on single files
  • Fine-tuned UI and CSS adjustments
  • Real-time completion for speed
  • Interactive refactoring with immediate feedback
  • Pair-programming feel for complex logic
  • Minimal context switching within a file
ℹ️
The combination allows you to choose the right tool for each task: big structural changes with Claude Code, precise tweaks with Cursor. This flexibility is what makes them the "ultimate combo."

Setting Up Cursor

Let's get Cursor installed and configured for optimal development.

Installation

Download and install Cursor from the official site:

Configuring Your AI Model

When Cursor launches for the first time, you'll be prompted to select an AI model.

  1. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the bottom left
  2. Navigate to Features → Models
  3. Select Claude 3.5 Sonnet or Claude Opus
💡
Claude Sonnet offers the best speed-to-quality ratio for most tasks. Choose Opus if you need deeper analysis or handling of longer contexts, though it's slower.

Essential Keyboard Shortcuts

Learn these Cursor shortcuts for faster workflows:

Cmd+K       — AI-powered code editing (describe what to change)
Cmd+L       — Open AI chat panel
Cmd+A       — Show all AI suggestions
Cmd+Shift+E — Execute editor commands
Cmd+/       — Toggle comment

To customize keybindings, edit ~/.cursor/settings.json:

Using Claude Code from the Terminal

Cursor's integrated terminal becomes your command center for Claude Code CLI operations.

Installation and Authentication

Install Claude Code CLI:

Authenticate with your API key:

Verify installation:

Opening the Terminal in Cursor

Open Cursor's integrated terminal:

Ctrl+` (backtick) — Opens/closes terminal
Or: View menu → Terminal

Now you can execute Claude Code commands directly:

💡
Position the terminal on the right side of Cursor's editor using the split view. This lets you see code changes in Cursor while Claude Code outputs results in the terminal—the perfect workflow loop.

Practical Workflow Examples

Workflow 1: Cursor for Tweaks → Claude Code for Testing & Fixing

Scenario: You've completed a major refactor and need to test individual functions and make targeted fixes.

Step 1: Claude Code performs broad refactoring

The AI analyzes your existing patterns and generates the complete refactored version.

Step 2: Cursor fine-tunes specific functions

With the file open in Cursor, press Cmd+K and write:

"Update the email validation to reject temporary email services"

Cursor applies the change instantly, and you can review it in real-time.

Step 3: Claude Code runs the test suite

Back in the terminal:

Review test results and see which assertions failed.

Step 4: Claude Code fixes failures

Step 5: Loop back to Step 2 if needed

This cycle ensures high-quality, well-tested code through collaborative AI assistance.

Workflow 2: Claude Code Scaffolds → Cursor Refines

Scenario: Building a new feature from scratch—generate structure with Claude Code, then polish with Cursor.

Step 1: Claude Code creates the foundation

Claude Code generates a complete, well-structured class with proper error handling.

Step 2: Cursor improves error messages

Open the generated file in Cursor. Use Cmd+K:

"Rewrite all error messages to be user-friendly. Include error codes so users can reference them in support tickets."

Step 3: Cursor adds documentation

Again with Cmd+K:

"Add comprehensive JSDoc comments to every public method. Include parameter descriptions, return types, and example usage."

Step 4: Claude Code generates tests

Step 5: Cursor validates the test structure

Open the test file and use Cmd+L to chat:

"Are these test cases comprehensive? What scenarios might we be missing?"

Cursor's AI responds with suggestions, which you can implement quickly.

Workflow 3: Claude Code Refactors → Cursor Validates Inline

Scenario: Modernizing existing code by extracting patterns into custom hooks.

Step 1: Claude Code analyzes patterns

Claude Code reports findings with specific locations.

Step 2: Claude Code performs refactoring

Step 3: Cursor validates each component

Open each refactored component in Cursor. Use Cmd+L:

"Does this custom hook properly handle cleanup? Are there any memory leaks?"

Walk through the logic and confirm everything is correct.

Step 4: Cursor optimizes performance

For performance-critical components, press Cmd+K:

"Add React.memo and useMemo where appropriate to prevent unnecessary re-renders"

Decision Framework: When to Use Each Tool

Use Cursor When:

  • Editing a single file — Logic adjustments, bug fixes within one component
  • Polishing UI/CSS — Fine-tuning styles, aligning layouts, tweaking colors
  • Interactive problem-solving — You need to explore solutions as you type
  • Quick iterations — Testing multiple approaches to a small problem
  • Learning code — Reading and understanding existing logic
  • Inline refactoring — Small, focused improvements within a file

Use Claude Code When:

  • Coordinating multiple files — Refactoring affects 3+ files across the codebase
  • Analyzing the project — Finding patterns, duplications, or improvement opportunities
  • Test creation & execution — Generating test suites and running full test passes
  • Git workflows — Creating commits, branches, and preparing PRs
  • Feature scaffolding — Generating complete, interconnected components from scratch
  • Search & replace at scale — Complex replacements across the entire codebase

Quick Decision Tree:

How many files will be changed?
├─ 1 file                → Use Cursor
├─ 2–3 related files     → Depends on similarity (high → Claude Code, low → Cursor)
└─ 4+ files             → Use Claude Code

MCP Integration: Extending Both Tools

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard that allows AI models to access external tools and data sources. Both Cursor and Claude Code support MCP, meaning they can share the same MCP servers and plugins.

Shared MCP Configuration

Create ~/.mcp-config.json (shared between Cursor and Claude Code):

Cursor configuration (~/.cursor/settings.json):

Claude Code configuration:

What This Enables:

  • Both tools query the same Git history
  • Shared filesystem access with consistent permissions
  • Database queries work identically in both environments
  • Web search results are consistent across tools
ℹ️
MCP is the bridge that makes Cursor and Claude Code feel like a unified system. When both tools have access to the same external resources, they can collaborate more effectively.

Tips & Best Practices

1. Create a Shared CLAUDE.md Context File

Place a CLAUDE.md file in your project root. Both Cursor and Claude Code will read it, ensuring consistent context:

Database Standards

  • Use Prisma migrations: npm run prisma:migrate
  • All transactions must use prisma.$transaction()
  • Never use raw SQL except for performance-critical queries
  • Index commonly-filtered fields

Testing Requirements

  • All new features require unit tests (>80% coverage)
  • Critical workflows require E2E tests
  • Test files: *.test.ts or *.test.tsx

Common Commands

Performance Targets

  • Largest bundle: <500KB gzipped
  • Core Web Vitals: LCP <2.5s, FID <100ms, CLS <0.1
  • Database queries: <100ms
  • API responses: <200ms

Known Gotchas

  1. Prisma relations require explicit includes—don't assume nested data
  2. NextAuth sessions live in cookies—be aware of size limits
  3. PostgreSQL arrays should be indexed with GIN indexes
  4. Tailwind classes must be static strings (no dynamic class names)

### 2. Distinguish Between `.cursorrules` and `CLAUDE.md`

Use `.cursorrules` for Cursor-specific behaviors:

.cursorrules — Cursor-specific preferences

When suggesting code completions:

  • Prioritize performance and memory efficiency
  • For React components, suggest memoization opportunities
  • Highlight potential accessibility issues
  • Suggest TypeScript types that are too permissive (e.g., any)

When the user presses Cmd+K:

  • Ask for clarification if the intent is ambiguous
  • Show before/after diffs for significant changes
  • Suggest related improvements in the same file

Keep general development rules in `CLAUDE.md` so both tools follow them.

### 3. Optimize Your Terminal Layout

The ideal Cursor setup uses a side-by-side layout:

┌──────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ Cursor Editor │ Integrated │ │ (code viewing │ Terminal │ │ & editing) │ (Claude Code CLI) │ │ │ │ └──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘


**Setup:**
1. Open terminal in Cursor: `Ctrl+``
2. Click the split button in the terminal header
3. Adjust pane widths using `View → Appearance → Panel Size`

**Benefits:**
- See code changes while Claude Code executes
- Monitor test results in real-time
- Context remains in one view
- No need to switch between windows

### 4. Create a Development Script

Create `dev.sh` to launch your optimal Cursor+Claude Code setup:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# dev.sh — Start your complete development environment

echo "Starting Claude Code + Cursor development environment..."

# Start dev server in background
npm run dev &
DEV_PID=$!

# Start Claude Code auth check
claude-code auth:check

# Open Cursor with the current directory
cursor .

# Cleanup on exit
trap "kill $DEV_PID" EXIT

echo "Development environment ready!"
echo "Use Claude Code in the integrated terminal to work at scale."

Run with: chmod +x dev.sh && ./dev.sh

5. Use .gitignore Effectively

Ensure Cursor and Claude Code don't interfere with version control:

Comparison with Other AI Editors

Cursor vs. Windsurf

| Aspect | Cursor | Windsurf | |--------|--------|----------| | Base | VS Code | VS Code | | AI Models | Claude, GPT-4 | Claude, GPT-4, Grok | | Chat | Yes | Yes | | Multi-file Edit | Good | Excellent | | Speed | Very Fast | Fast | | Learning Curve | Low | Medium |

Cursor is lighter and more responsive; Windsurf offers more sophisticated multi-file reasoning.

Cursor vs. GitHub Copilot

Copilot:    VS Code Extension (augments existing editor)
Cursor:     Standalone Editor (AI is native)

Key difference: Copilot is a plugin; Cursor is the editor itself. Copilot excels at completion; Cursor excels at interactive editing.

Cursor vs. Claude Code CLI

Cursor:     Real-time visual feedback, immediate iteration
Claude Code: Powerful analysis, multi-file coordination, CI/CD automation

They're complementary, not competing. Use them together as this guide demonstrates.

Advanced Patterns

Pattern 1: Test-Driven Development

  1. Claude Code creates initial feature scaffold and test file
  2. Cursor iteratively develops the feature while tests are visible
  3. Claude Code runs full test suite and identifies gaps
  4. Cursor adds edge case handling

Pattern 2: Code Review Automation

Pattern 3: Documentation Generation

Conclusion

Cursor and Claude Code represent two complementary approaches to AI-assisted development:

  • Cursor for interactive, real-time editing with deep single-file context
  • Claude Code for strategic, large-scale changes with full project vision
  • Together they form a system where each tool does what it does best

Start with one simple project. Use Cursor for an afternoon of polishing a component. Use Claude Code for refactoring a feature across files. Within days, you'll intuitively know which tool to reach for, and your development speed will exceed what either tool alone could provide.

💡
The true power emerges not from using either tool perfectly, but from understanding when to switch between them. That muscle memory takes practice, but the payoff is substantial.

Next Steps:

  1. Install both Cursor and Claude Code
  2. Create a test project or use an existing one
  3. Follow Workflow 1, 2, or 3 above
  4. Adjust based on your team's needs and preferences
  5. Share your workflows with teammates—they'll want to adopt this too

Happy coding with your new AI dev team!

en.title: File Management and Desktop Operations — Organize, Convert, and Batch Process Files with Cowork
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Cowork for file management, format conversion, and batch processing. Organize your desktop and automate file operations with ease.
en.content:
What is Cowork?

Cowork is Anthropic's desktop application that brings Claude's capabilities directly to your file system and computer. It allows you to manage files, perform batch operations, convert formats, and automate repetitive desktop tasks without leaving your workspace.

ℹ️
Cowork runs locally on your computer and has access to your file system. You maintain full control over which directories Claude can access and what operations it can perform.

Installing Cowork

System Requirements

  • macOS 11+, Windows 10+, or Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+)
  • 4GB RAM minimum
  • 500MB free disk space
  • Anthropic account with API key

Installation Steps

  1. Download the Cowork application from anthropic.com
  2. Install using the platform-specific installer
  3. Launch Cowork from your applications folder
  4. Authenticate with your Anthropic account
  5. Grant folder access to the directories you want to work with

Understanding Folder Access

One of Cowork's key security features is granular folder access control. You explicitly choose which directories Claude can access.

Requesting Folder Access

When you ask Claude to work with files:

  1. Claude requests permission to access a specific folder
  2. A dialog appears asking you to confirm
  3. You can browse and select the folder
  4. Claude gains access only to that folder and its contents
  5. You can revoke access at any time in settings

Best Practices for Folder Access

  • Grant minimal access: Only allow access to folders you need Claude to work with
  • Organize your files: Keep related files in the same folder
  • Avoid sensitive folders: Don't grant access to credential or configuration directories
  • Review permissions regularly: Check which folders Claude can access periodically

Common File Operations

Reading Files

Ask Claude to read and analyze your files:

Read the CSV file "sales_data.csv" and summarize the key metrics.

Claude can read:

  • Text files (.txt, .md)
  • Code files (.py, .js, .java, etc.)
  • Structured data (.csv, .json)
  • Documents (.pdf, .docx)

Creating and Writing Files

Claude can create new files in authorized folders:

Create a Python script called "analysis.py" that processes the data.

Claude will:

  • Create the file with specified content
  • Use proper formatting and syntax
  • Place the file in the correct location
  • Show you the content before saving

Copying and Moving Files

Organize your files by asking Claude to reorganize them:

Copy all PNG files from the Downloads folder to a new "Images" subfolder.

Renaming Files in Bulk

Claude can rename multiple files at once:

Rename all PDF files in this folder to include today's date in the filename.
Change "document.pdf" to "document_2026-03-09.pdf"

File Format Conversion

One of Cowork's most powerful features is batch file format conversion. Claude can convert between many formats automatically.

Supported Conversions

| From | To | Common Use Cases | |------|----|----| | CSV | JSON, Excel, PDF | Data migration, API integration | | Markdown | HTML, PDF, DOCX | Publishing, documentation | | Images | PNG, JPG, WEBP | Compression, format standardization | | JSON | CSV, Excel | Data analysis, spreadsheet import | | Audio | MP3, WAV, OGG | Compression, compatibility |

Example: CSV to JSON

Convert "sales_data.csv" to JSON format.
Keep the structure flat and use descriptive field names.
Save as "sales_data.json".

Claude will handle the conversion automatically while preserving data integrity.

Example: Markdown to PDF

Convert all .md files in this folder to PDF.
Apply professional formatting with a standard font.
Save PDFs with the same filenames.

Batch Format Conversion

Convert multiple files at once:

I have 20 TIFF images that need to be converted to JPG format.
Compress them to 80% quality and keep them in the same folder.
Add "_compressed" to each filename.

Batch Processing Operations

Finding and Filtering Files

Claude can locate specific files based on criteria:

Find all Excel files modified in the last 7 days.
List their sizes and creation dates.

Organizing by Type

Automatically sort files into categorized folders:

Organize my Downloads folder.
Create subfolders for: Documents, Images, Videos, Archives, Others.
Move files into appropriate folders based on their type.

Cleaning Up Duplicates

Find and manage duplicate files:

Find duplicate files in my Documents folder (same size and content).
List them and show which ones to keep.

Extracting Data from Files

Process multiple files and extract information:

Scan all PDF invoices in this folder.
Extract the invoice number, date, and total amount.
Create a CSV summary with the results.

Advanced File Management Tasks

Image Batch Operations

I have 50 photos from a vacation.
1. Resize them all to 1920x1080
2. Add a watermark with "© 2026"
3. Convert to JPG at 90% quality
4. Save in a new "processed" folder

Claude can handle these multi-step operations efficiently.

Document Processing

I have a folder of Word documents that need to be:
1. Converted to markdown
2. Have headings reformatted to use # syntax
3. Have code blocks marked with ```
4. Saved as .md files

Data Extraction

Extract all email addresses from PDFs in this folder.
Also extract phone numbers in format (XXX) XXX-XXXX.
Create a CSV with columns: source_file, email, phone.

Workspace Setup and Organization

Creating a Project Workspace

Set up a dedicated workspace for a project:

Create a new folder structure for my "Website Redesign" project:
- /designs (for mockups and screenshots)
- /assets (for images, icons, fonts)
- /content (for text and markdown)
- /exports (for final deliverables)

Organizing by Date

Maintain chronological organization:

Sort my archive by year and month.
Create folders: 2024/01, 2024/02, ... 2026/03
Move files into appropriate date folders based on creation date.

Document Templates

Create reusable templates for repeated tasks:

Create a project template folder with:
- README.md (project description)
- CHANGELOG.md (version history)
- TODO.md (task list)
- .gitignore (standard git ignores)

Security Considerations

Protecting Sensitive Files

  • Never grant access to folders containing passwords or credentials
  • Use encryption for sensitive documents before processing
  • Review files that Claude will process before sharing access
  • Limit scope: Give access only to necessary folders

Best Practices

  1. Keep credentials separate: Store API keys and passwords outside Cowork's access
  2. Audit operations: Review significant file operations before they're executed
  3. Backup important files: Keep backups before bulk operations
  4. Revoke access: Remove folder permissions when no longer needed
  5. Monitor activity: Check the operation log periodically
⚠️
Cowork performs real file operations. Always review what Claude will do before confirming batch operations that involve deletion or large-scale changes.

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Permission Denied Errors

Problem: "Permission denied" when accessing a folder

Solution:

  • Grant folder access in Cowork settings
  • Check file/folder permissions on your system
  • Ensure you have read/write permissions

File Encoding Issues

Problem: Text files display incorrectly

Solution:

  • Ensure files are UTF-8 encoded
  • Try converting to UTF-8 before processing
  • Check file extensions match actual format

Conversion Failures

Problem: File conversion doesn't complete

Solution:

  • Check file integrity (file isn't corrupted)
  • Ensure sufficient disk space
  • Try smaller batches if processing many files
  • Check file format compatibility

Performance Tips

  1. Work with reasonable batch sizes: Process 50-100 files at a time, not thousands
  2. Close unneeded applications: Free up system resources for conversions
  3. Use SSD when possible: Faster storage improves performance
  4. Monitor system resources: Watch CPU and disk usage during large operations
  5. Split large projects: Break into smaller, manageable tasks

Next Steps

  • Set up your first project workspace
  • Try a simple file format conversion
  • Organize a folder of miscellaneous files
  • Automate a repetitive file management task you do regularly

en.title: Hooks and Automation — Automate Your Workflow with Claude Code Hooks
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Master Claude Code hooks to automate your development workflow. Learn to use PreToolUse, PostToolUse, and Notification hooks with configuration examples.
en.content:
What Are Hooks?

Hooks in Claude Code are event-driven scripts that execute automatically at specific points in your workflow. They let you automate repetitive tasks, inject custom logic into Claude's tool execution, and extend Claude Code's functionality beyond built-in capabilities.

ℹ️
Hooks are configured in the `.claude/settings.json` file at your project root. They run locally in your development environment, giving you full control over custom automation.

Types of Hooks

Claude Code supports three main hook types:

  1. PreToolUse — Runs before Claude executes a tool
  2. PostToolUse — Runs after Claude executes a tool
  3. Notification — Sends alerts and notifications to external services

Setting Up Your First Hook

Creating .claude/settings.json

First, create the configuration file at your project root:

Basic Configuration

PreToolUse Hooks

PreToolUse hooks run before Claude executes a tool, allowing you to validate parameters, add context, or prevent dangerous operations.

Use Cases for PreToolUse

  • Validation: Check parameters before file operations
  • Security: Prevent commits to critical branches
  • Logging: Record what Claude is about to do
  • Modification: Add or modify tool parameters

Example: File Write Validation

Create scripts/pre-write-validation.js:

Configuration for File Write Validation

Example: Git Branch Protection

Create scripts/pre-git-commit.js:

PostToolUse Hooks

PostToolUse hooks execute after Claude completes a tool operation, allowing you to log results, trigger notifications, or perform cleanup operations.

Use Cases for PostToolUse

  • Logging: Record successful operations
  • Notifications: Alert team members of changes
  • Cleanup: Remove temporary files or cache
  • Validation: Verify operations completed correctly

Example: Commit Notification

Create scripts/post-commit-notification.js:

Example: Build Automation

Create scripts/post-test-automation.js:

Configuration for Build Automation

Notification Hooks

Notification hooks send alerts to external services like Slack, Discord, or email when specific events occur.

Example: Slack Notifications

Create scripts/slack-notifier.js:

Configuration for Slack Notifications

Complete .claude/settings.json Example

Best Practices for Hooks

  1. Keep hooks fast: Long-running hooks slow down your workflow
  2. Handle errors gracefully: Hooks should fail safely
  3. Log important actions: Make hooks auditable
  4. Test thoroughly: Test hooks before deploying to production
  5. Document your hooks: Include comments explaining what each does
  6. Use environment variables: Store secrets in .env files
  7. Limit scope: Apply hooks only to the tools that need them
⚠️
Hooks run locally in your environment. Never use hooks to execute untrusted code or operations you don't fully understand.

Debugging Hooks

Check Hook Execution

Common Issues

| Issue | Solution | |-------|----------| | Hook not running | Check .claude/settings.json for syntax errors | | Hook throws error | Test hook script independently with node | | Parameters not passed | Verify hook handler signature matches expected format | | Performance issues | Profile hook execution time, consider async optimization |

Advanced Hook Patterns

Conditional Execution

Chaining Multiple Hooks

Next Steps

  • Create your first hook for file validation
  • Set up notifications for your team
  • Integrate hooks with your CI/CD pipeline
  • Build custom automation specific to your workflow

en.title: Implementing Streaming Responses — Real-time Responses with the Claude API
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Master streaming responses with the Claude API. Learn to implement server-sent events, handle stream events, and build real-time user interfaces.
en.content:
What is Streaming?

Streaming allows you to receive Claude's response token-by-token in real time, rather than waiting for the complete response. This provides immediate feedback to users and is essential for building responsive applications.

ℹ️
Streaming is particularly valuable for: - Chat applications (show response as it's being generated) - Long-form content (news articles, reports) - Real-time dashboards - Mobile applications with limited bandwidth - Improving perceived response time

Understanding Server-Sent Events (SSE)

Streaming responses use the Server-Sent Events (SSE) protocol, which sends data as a stream of events from the server to the client.

SSE Format

data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":0,"content_block":{"type":"text"}}

data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"Hello"}}

data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":" world"}}

data: {"type":"message_stop"}

Each event is a JSON object with a type field indicating what kind of event it is.

Python Streaming Implementation

Basic Streaming Example

Detailed Event Handling

For more control, you can handle individual events:

Collecting Full Response During Streaming

TypeScript/JavaScript Streaming

Using the Official SDK

Manual Event Processing

Web-Based Streaming (Frontend)

Fetch API with Streaming

React Streaming Component

Stream Event Types

Supported Events

Error Handling in Streams

Handling Stream Errors

Timeout Handling

UI/UX Best Practices

Progressive Display

Show response as it arrives:

Loading Indicators

Handling Network Issues

Performance Tips

  1. Always use streaming for long responses — Better UX and reduced perceived latency
  2. Implement debouncing — Don't update UI on every token for very fast streams
  3. Use connection pooling — Reuse HTTP connections for multiple streams
  4. Monitor stream health — Track errors and retry failures
  5. Set reasonable timeouts — Prevent hanging connections
⚠️
Don't buffer the entire response before showing it to users. Stream tokens as they arrive for the best user experience.

en.title: Introduction to Prompt Engineering — Techniques for Designing Effective Claude Prompts
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Master the art of prompt engineering. Learn proven techniques like system prompts, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought reasoning, and XML tags to dramatically improve Claude's response quality.
en.content:
What is Prompt Engineering?

Prompt engineering is the practice of designing and refining prompts to get better, more accurate responses from Claude. Small changes in how you phrase a question can lead to dramatically different results. By understanding Claude's capabilities and limitations, you can craft prompts that consistently produce high-quality outputs.

ℹ️
Great prompt engineering is not about being cryptic or complex. It's about being clear, specific, and structured to help Claude understand exactly what you need.

System Prompts

A system prompt sets the context and behavior for the entire conversation. It's processed before your actual query and acts as invisible instructions that guide Claude's responses.

Using System Prompts

System Prompt:
You are a technical writing expert specializing in software documentation.
Always structure responses with clear headings and concise explanations.
Use examples to illustrate complex concepts.
Avoid jargon without first defining it.

System prompts are particularly effective for:

  • Establishing a professional tone or personality
  • Defining the assistant's role or expertise
  • Specifying output format requirements
  • Setting constraints or guidelines

In Claude.ai, you can set system prompts in the project settings. In the API, use the system parameter in your request.

Few-Shot Examples (In-Context Learning)

Few-shot examples teach Claude how to respond by demonstrating the desired pattern before asking it to solve your actual problem. This technique is more effective than lengthy explanations.

Example: Text Classification

Here are examples of sentiment classification:

Input: "This product is amazing! I love it."
Classification: POSITIVE

Input: "It broke after one week. Very disappointed."
Classification: NEGATIVE

Input: "It's okay, nothing special but it works."
Classification: NEUTRAL

Now classify this: "The customer service was unhelpful and rude."
Classification:

Few-shot prompting works because Claude learns patterns from concrete examples. It's especially useful when:

  • Your task has specific formatting requirements
  • You need consistent categorization across many items
  • You want to define custom categories or labels
  • Standard instructions aren't producing the desired format

Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

Chain-of-thought prompting asks Claude to explain its reasoning step-by-step before providing a final answer. This technique improves accuracy, especially for complex problem-solving and reasoning tasks.

Without Chain-of-Thought

Q: A train travels 120 miles at 40 mph. How long is the trip?
A: 3 hours

With Chain-of-Thought

Q: A train travels 120 miles at 40 mph. How long is the trip?

Think through this step by step:
1. Identify what we know
2. Write the relevant formula
3. Perform the calculation
4. State your answer

A:

Claude will naturally explain each step, leading to more accurate and verifiable results. This is invaluable for:

  • Complex calculations and reasoning
  • Decision-making processes
  • Debugging and troubleshooting
  • Academic or technical problems
💡
Chain-of-thought is also known as "thinking out loud." Asking Claude to verbalize its reasoning helps catch errors and makes the response more trustworthy.

XML Tags for Structure

XML tags help you organize complex prompts and clearly separate different parts of your request. They act as visual delimiters that make the prompt easier to understand and parse.

Structured Request with XML Tags

<task>
Write a product description for a wireless headphone.
</task>

<requirements>
- Maximum 150 words
- Target audience: professionals
- Emphasize battery life and sound quality
- Include one key technical specification
</requirements>

<tone>
Professional but approachable
</tone>

<format>
Start with a headline, followed by two paragraphs.
</format>

XML tags are effective because:

  • They clearly separate different types of information
  • Claude can easily identify the structure of your request
  • They work particularly well for complex or multi-part requests
  • They improve consistency when used across multiple prompts

Output Formatting Specifications

Being explicit about your desired output format significantly improves result quality. Instead of hoping Claude guesses the right format, tell it exactly what you want.

Specifying JSON Output

Generate 5 creative business name ideas for a tech startup.
Return as JSON with this structure:

{
  "names": [
    {
      "name": "string",
      "reasoning": "string"
    }
  ]
}

Specifying Markdown Tables

Compare Python, JavaScript, and Go across 5 dimensions.
Format as a markdown table with languages as columns and dimensions as rows.

Specifying Lists and Bullet Points

List the top 10 productivity tips for remote workers.
Format as a numbered list with brief one-line descriptions.
Each item should have a practical action step.

Combining Techniques: A Complete Example

You are an expert Python code reviewer. Your role is to identify bugs,
suggest optimizations, and explain improvements clearly.

Here's how you should review code:

1. Identify any bugs or logical errors
2. Suggest performance improvements
3. Recommend best practices
4. Explain each suggestion in simple terms

Format your review as:
- **Bugs Found:** [list any issues]
- **Performance:** [optimization suggestions]
- **Best Practices:** [recommendations]

Review this code:
[code snippet here]

Think through potential issues step by step before providing your review.

This example combines:

  • System prompt (role definition)
  • Few-shot examples (format demonstration)
  • Output specification (structured format)
  • Chain-of-thought (step-by-step thinking)

Testing and Iteration

Prompt engineering is not a one-shot process. The best prompts are refined through testing:

  1. Start simple — Begin with a clear, basic request
  2. Test variations — Try different phrasings and structures
  3. Evaluate quality — Does the output meet your needs?
  4. Iterate — Refine based on results
  5. Document — Save your best prompts for reuse

Keep a collection of prompts that work well for your common tasks. Over time, you'll develop an intuition for what works with Claude.

Common Pitfalls to Avoid

  • Being too vague: "Write something about AI" is less effective than "Write a 300-word beginner's guide to prompt engineering"
  • Overcomplicating: Lengthy prompts aren't always better. Clarity beats verbosity
  • Assuming context: Don't assume Claude knows your specific terminology or background
  • Inconsistent formatting: When asking for structured output, be precise about format
  • Neglecting tone: Tone matters. Specify "formal," "friendly," "technical," etc.

Next Steps

  • Experiment with system prompts in your Claude.ai projects
  • Try few-shot examples for repetitive categorization tasks
  • Use chain-of-thought for complex reasoning
  • Build a personal library of effective prompts for your workflows

Want to study prompt engineering more systematically? Check out our recommended books for learning AI & LLMs, including titles focused on prompt engineering techniques.

en.title: Practical Claude Code Workflows — From Project Setup to Deployment
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Real-world Claude Code workflows for developers. Project initialization, code review, test automation, CI/CD integration, debugging, and more.
en.content:
