Claude Code is more than a code generation tool—it's your development partner. This guide covers practical workflows you can use immediately in real projects, from initialization through deployment. Master these techniques to unlock Claude Code's full potential.

Using Claude Code as Your Development Partner

Effective collaboration with Claude Code requires understanding some key principles.

Defining Project Context with CLAUDE.md

The foundation of effective Claude Code usage is a clear project context. Create a CLAUDE.md file in your project root to document project structure, design principles, and coding standards. This enables Claude Code to generate consistent, on-brand code.

💡
CLAUDE.md becomes the standard reference for Claude Code. Update it as your project evolves to maintain consistency across the entire codebase.

Project Initialization Workflow

Project setup is one of Claude Code's greatest strengths. Here's how to leverage it effectively.

Step 1: Express Requirements Clearly

Claude Code doesn't just install dependencies—it generates a complete project structure including configuration, sample components, tests, and documentation.

Step 2: Verify and Customize

Step 3: Verify Development Environment

⚠️
Always manually start the development server after initialization and test the setup. Verify that Claude Code's generated code works in your environment.

Code Review Workflow

Claude Code becomes a capable code reviewer when reviewing changes to existing projects.

Step 1: Request Review of Changes

Step 2: Implement Improvement Suggestions

Step 3: Validate with Tests

Git Integration Best Practices

Test-Driven Development

Test-driven development (TDD) pairs exceptionally well with Claude Code.

Step 1: Define Test Cases

Step 2: Implement Test Code

Step 3: Implement Production Code

Improve Coverage

💡
TDD with Claude Code clarifies requirements upfront. Claude Code implements based on tests, eliminating specification mismatch and reducing bugs.

Debugging Workflow

Claude Code becomes your debugging partner when production issues arise.

Step 1: Collect Error Logs and Reproduction Steps

Step 2: Identify and Fix Root Cause

Step 3: Create Regression Tests

Step 4: Verify Locally

⚠️
Production bugs are urgent, but always test thoroughly in your local environment before deploying fixes.

Refactoring Workflow

As codebases grow, refactoring becomes necessary. Claude Code streamlines this process.

Step 1: Identify Refactoring Opportunities

Step 2: Create Refactoring Plan

Step 3: Implement in Phases

Step 4: Test and Validate

After each refactoring phase, run the full test suite.

💡
Refactor in small, incremental steps. Large changes make bug identification difficult.

Documentation Generation

Claude Code can automate documentation that's often neglected.

Generate README and Setup Guide

Auto-Generate API Documentation

Add JSDoc Comments

Maintain Documentation

Multi-File Editing

Claude Code excels at coordinating changes across multiple files.

Implementing a Feature Across Files

Complex Feature Refactoring

⚠️
For multi-file changes, run your complete test suite to catch regressions.

CI/CD Integration

Integrating Claude Code with CI/CD pipelines multiplies its effectiveness.

Build GitHub Actions Workflow

Configure Deployment Pipeline

Analyze Test Results

Handy One-Liners

Essential Claude Code commands for daily development.

Frequently Used Commands

Best Practices — Effective Prompting

Effective prompt crafting is essential for maximum Claude Code effectiveness.

1. Provide Sufficient Context

Poor:

claude "Fix the authentication bug"

Better:

claude "There's a bug in the authentication flow.

Error:
\`\`\`
TypeError: user is undefined
at login (src/auth.ts:42)
\`\`\`

Reproduction:
1. Go to /login
2. Enter test@example.com with wrong password
3. Click login
4. After 3 failed attempts, error appears

Current code:
\`\`\`typescript
$(cat src/auth.ts)
\`\`\`

Fix and ensure:
- Clear error messages shown to users
- Failed attempts are logged
- Account locked after 5 attempts"

2. Quantify Requirements

3. Provide File Content Directly

4. Build Context Incrementally

5. Use Templates and Examples

6. Specify Constraints

💡
Include not just "what" and "how" but also "why" and constraints. This helps Claude Code propose the most appropriate solutions.

7. Incorporate Staged Validation

8. Include Negative Constraints

Context Management — Large Project Usage

For large projects, effective context management is critical.

Comprehensive CLAUDE.md Template

src/ ├── app/ # Next.js app router ├── components/ # React components ├── hooks/ # Custom hooks ├── lib/ # Utilities ├── services/ # API/external services ├── types/ # TypeScript types └── middleware/ # Express/Next.js middleware


## Coding Standards
1. **Language**: TypeScript 5+, ES2023
2. **Formatter**: Prettier (--print-width 100)
3. **File Naming**: kebab-case files, camelCase exports
4. **Components**: Function-based, React 18 hooks only
5. **Styling**: Tailwind CSS + CSS Modules

## Component Design
- Single Responsibility Principle
- Props must be TypeScript typed
- Jotai/Redux Toolkit for state
- TanStack Query for API calls

## Testing Standards
- Unit: Vitest + Testing Library (80%+ coverage)
- Integration: Playwright
- E2E: Cypress
- Minimum: 85% coverage target

## Deployment
- Staging: Vercel preview
- Production: AWS EC2 / Vercel
- Database: PostgreSQL (RDS)
- Monitoring: Sentry, DataDog

## Security
- HTTPS only
- CORS properly configured
- SQL injection prevention
- XSS protection (CSP)
- CSRF token validation

Context Switching Strategy

For large projects, maintain separate contexts for different modules.

Useful File Reference Patterns

Summary

To maximize Claude Code effectiveness:

  1. Define Clear Context with CLAUDE.md — This is the foundation
  2. Use Incremental Approaches — Break large tasks into small units
  3. Always Validate with Tests — Verify Claude Code's output
  4. Write Detailed Prompts — Include requirements, constraints, and context
  5. Maintain Feedback Loops — Refine prompts based on results

Mastering these workflows transforms Claude Code into a trusted development partner that significantly accelerates your development velocity.

Next Steps: Start using Claude Code on real projects. Begin with simple tasks (boilerplate generation, bug fixes) and progress to complex ones (architecture design, large refactorings). Each successful interaction teaches Claude Code more about your project and preferences.

en.title: Projects and Memory — Managing Context with Claude.ai's Projects Feature
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude.ai's Projects feature to manage context, add custom instructions, upload files, and collaborate with your team. Master organization for better conversations.
en.content:
What Are Projects?

Projects in Claude.ai are organized workspaces that let you manage conversations, instructions, and files in one place. Instead of starting fresh with every conversation, Projects retain context, instructions, and files — making your workflow more efficient and your conversations more personalized.

ℹ️
Projects are available on all Claude.ai plans, including the free tier. Each user gets access to project creation and management.

Why Use Projects?

Traditional Approach (Without Projects)

  • Each conversation starts from scratch
  • No memory of previous interactions
  • Frequently repeating instructions and context
  • Files scattered across different conversations

Project-Based Approach

  • Persistent context across conversations
  • Custom instructions stored and reused
  • Centralized file management
  • Better organization and team collaboration

Creating Your First Project

Step-by-Step Setup

  1. Go to Claude.ai and log in to your account
  2. Click "New Project" from the sidebar
  3. Enter project details:
    • Project Name: Choose a descriptive name (e.g., "Blog Writing", "Research Q1 2026")
    • Description: Optional, but helpful for remembering the project's purpose
  4. Start your first conversation within the project

Once created, all conversations in this project will be organized together, and you can add custom instructions that apply to the entire project.

Adding Project Instructions

Project instructions are custom guidelines that apply to all conversations within that project. They're perfect for defining your writing style, technical preferences, or role-specific behavior.

Example: Writing Project Instructions

# Writing Project Instructions

## Tone and Style
- Use clear, concise language
- Avoid jargon unless necessary
- Write for a general audience
- Include examples to illustrate points

## Structure
- Begin with a hook or question
- Use descriptive headings
- End with actionable takeaways
- Keep paragraphs to 3-4 sentences

## Formatting
- Use markdown formatting
- Include links when relevant
- Add bullet points for lists
💡
Project instructions are like a standing order. Claude reads them at the start of each conversation, so you don't need to repeat them in every message.

Example: Development Project Instructions

# Development Project Instructions

## Technology Stack
- Next.js 16 with App Router
- TypeScript (strict mode)
- Tailwind CSS v4
- PostgreSQL for the database

## Coding Conventions
- Use functional components with hooks
- Write TypeScript interfaces for all props
- Include JSDoc comments for exported functions
- Follow Airbnb style guide for spacing and naming

## Testing Requirements
- Write unit tests with Vitest
- Aim for 80% code coverage
- Test both happy paths and edge cases

Uploading and Managing Files

Projects let you attach documents, code files, datasets, and other resources that Claude can reference throughout all your conversations in that project.

How to Upload Files

  1. Open a project and start a conversation
  2. Click the attachment icon (paperclip) in the chat box
  3. Select files from your computer
  4. Supported formats:
    • Documents: PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD
    • Code: PY, JS, TS, JAVA, CPP, C#, etc.
    • Data: CSV, JSON, XLSX
    • Images: PNG, JPG, GIF
    • Archives: ZIP, TAR.GZ

Once uploaded, Claude can reference these files in any conversation within the project.

File Management Best Practices

  • Keep files organized: Use descriptive filenames
  • Remove outdated files: Archive or delete files no longer needed
  • Version important files: Consider including dates (e.g., "requirements_2026-03-01.md")
  • Respect file size limits: Large files may affect performance
⚠️
Some file types have processing limits. If your file is very large (>10MB), consider splitting it or compressing it before upload.

The Memory Feature

The Memory feature automatically remembers key details about you, your preferences, and your project goals. Unlike project files (which you explicitly upload), Memory learns and evolves based on your interactions.

What Memory Captures

  • Your communication style preferences
  • Technical preferences and conventions you use
  • Recurring goals and priorities
  • People and projects you frequently discuss
  • Naming conventions and terminology you prefer

How Memory Works

  1. Claude observes patterns across your conversations
  2. Memory updates automatically based on your interactions
  3. Applied to future conversations without you needing to repeat information
  4. You can review and edit memory entries manually

Managing Your Memory

You can view and edit your memory at any time:

  1. Open a project
  2. Click "Memory" or "Project Settings"
  3. View stored information about your preferences
  4. Edit or delete entries as needed
ℹ️
Memory is project-specific. Each project maintains its own memory, allowing you to have different preferences for different projects.

Memory Examples

Writing Project Memory:

  • Prefers British English spelling
  • Likes numbered lists over bullet points
  • Targets audience: business professionals
  • Writing style: conversational but professional

Development Project Memory:

  • Uses camelCase for JavaScript variables
  • Prefers TypeScript over JavaScript
  • Testing framework: Vitest
  • Database: PostgreSQL

Sharing Projects with Your Team

Projects can be shared with team members, making collaboration seamless.

How to Share a Project

  1. Open the project
  2. Click "Share" or "Project Settings"
  3. Add team members by email address
  4. Set permissions:
    • View only: Can read conversations and files
    • Edit: Can modify conversations and add files
    • Admin: Full control including sharing and deletion

Team Collaboration Benefits

  • Shared context: Everyone understands the project background
  • Unified conversations: Team members can continue each other's discussions
  • Consistent instructions: Project instructions apply to all team members
  • File access: All team members can reference shared files

Organizing Multiple Projects

As you create more projects, organization becomes important.

Project Organization Strategy

  1. By Type: Separate projects for writing, development, research, etc.
  2. By Client/Team: Dedicated projects for each client or team
  3. By Time Period: Quarterly or yearly projects (e.g., "Q1 2026 Marketing")
  4. By Topic: Projects grouped by subject matter

Naming Convention Tips

  • Use clear, descriptive names
  • Include dates if time-sensitive (e.g., "Book Draft 2026-Q1")
  • Avoid generic names like "Project 1" or "Misc"
  • Keep names concise (3-5 words maximum)

Project Settings and Customization

Each project has customization options to suit your needs.

Available Settings

  • Project name and description — Update anytime
  • Custom instructions — Define behavior and preferences
  • File attachments — Upload and manage resources
  • Sharing settings — Control who can access
  • Archive/Delete — Manage inactive projects

Best Practices for Project Management

  1. Start with a clear purpose — Know what the project is for before creating it
  2. Write detailed instructions — The more specific, the better Claude's responses
  3. Keep files current — Remove outdated or irrelevant files regularly
  4. Review memory periodically — Ensure stored preferences are accurate
  5. Share strategically — Only share with people who need access
  6. Name consistently — Use clear, descriptive project names

Example Projects to Create

Writing Workflow

- Project: "Blog Writing 2026"
- Instructions: Tone guidelines, SEO preferences, target audience
- Files: Brand guidelines, previous articles, keywords list

Software Development

- Project: "E-commerce Platform"
- Instructions: Tech stack, coding conventions, testing requirements
- Files: API documentation, database schema, design specs

Research and Analysis

- Project: "Market Research Q1"
- Instructions: Analysis framework, output format preferences
- Files: Datasets, competitor analyses, market reports

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Claude Isn't Remembering Previous Conversations

  • Solution: Make sure you're in the same project. Different projects don't share memory.
  • Check that project instructions are properly saved.

Files Won't Upload

  • Solution: Check file size and format compatibility.
  • Try a smaller file to test, or split large files.

Memory Isn't Updating

  • Solution: Memory updates automatically, but may take a few conversations.
  • You can manually edit memory in Project Settings.

Next Steps

  • Create your first project based on your primary workflow
  • Set up custom instructions that reflect your preferences
  • Upload key files and documents you frequently reference
  • Invite team members to collaborate on shared projects

en.title: skills.sh Complete Guide — How to Install Skills for Claude
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to install and manage skills from the skills.sh marketplace for Claude Code and Cowork. From discovery to publishing your own skills.
en.content:
import { Callout } from '@/components/ui/callout';

What is skills.sh?

skills.sh is an open-source agent skills marketplace that extends the capabilities of Claude Code, Cowork, and other AI agents. Built on the open standard defined by agentskills.io, skills.sh provides a unified ecosystem where developers can discover, install, and publish skills that add new functionality to their AI-powered workflows.

As of March 2026, the skills marketplace hosts thousands of verified skills spanning creative applications (art, music, design), technical tasks (testing, code review, git automation), and enterprise workflows (communications, branding, and data processing).

ℹ️
Skills are fundamentally different from slash commands. Skills are model-invoked—Claude automatically decides when to use them based on context. Slash commands, by contrast, are user-invoked—you explicitly type the command to trigger them. This means skills enable more intelligent, context-aware automation in your workflows without requiring explicit user action.

Understanding Skills Architecture

A skill is a modular capability that contains:

  • SKILL.md file: A Markdown file with YAML frontmatter containing metadata and instructions
  • Metadata: Information about the skill's purpose, version, author, and triggers
  • Instructions: Detailed guidance for how the agent should use this skill
  • Optional resources: Supporting files like scripts, references, and assets

Skills are stored in two possible locations:

  • Global installation: ~/.claude/skills/ for skills available across all projects
  • Project-specific installation: ./.claude/skills/ within a project directory

The modular design allows agents to automatically load relevant skills when needed, creating a seamless and context-aware experience.

Installing the Skills CLI

The Skills CLI is the command-line tool for managing skills. Install it using npm:

Or use npx to run commands without installing:

💡
If you're using Node.js, you likely have npm already installed. Verify your setup with `npm --version` and `node --version` before proceeding.

Discovering and Installing Skills

Finding Skills

Search the marketplace for skills matching your needs:

Common search examples:

Each search result displays the skill name, description, version, and author information. This helps you evaluate whether a skill matches your specific requirements.

Installing Skills

Once you've identified a skill, install it with:

Installation examples:

Global installation (-g flag) makes the skill available in all your projects, while project-level installation stores skills only in the current project's .claude/skills/ directory.

⚠️
Always review a skill's source and description before installation. While the marketplace contains community-verified skills, ensure you trust the author and understand what the skill does in your workflows.

Managing Your Skills

Checking for Updates

View all installed skills and check for available updates:

This command displays which skills have newer versions available and provides guidance on updating them.

Updating Skills

Keep your skills current with the latest improvements and bug fixes:

This updates all installed skills to their latest versions. You can also update individual skills by specifying the skill name.

Listing Installed Skills

View all skills currently installed in your project:

Removing Skills

Remove skills you no longer use:

Or use the interactive removal tool:

This provides a menu to select which skills to uninstall.

Understanding the SKILL.md Format

Every skill is defined by a SKILL.md file containing YAML frontmatter and Markdown content.

Frontmatter Structure

Frontmatter fields:

| Field | Description | Required | |-------|-------------|----------| | name | Skill display name | Yes | | description | Brief explanation of skill purpose | Yes | | version | Semantic version number | Yes | | author | Skill creator/maintainer | Yes | | license | Open source license type | Yes | | triggers | Keywords that activate this skill | No | | metadata | Additional categorization and tagging | No |

Markdown Content

After the frontmatter, write clear instructions for how the agent should use this skill:

💡
Keep SKILL.md instructions concise and under 5,000 tokens. Focus on the essential guidance that will help Claude make better decisions in your workflows.

Creating Custom Skills

Initializing a New Skill

Create a new skill project structure:

This generates:

  • SKILL.md with template frontmatter and content
  • Optional directories for scripts, references, and assets
  • A basic project structure

Skill Development Workflow

  1. Define the Purpose: Clearly state what task or domain your skill addresses
  2. Write Frontmatter: Complete all required metadata fields
  3. Document Instructions: Provide clear, actionable guidance for the AI agent
  4. Add Resources (optional): Include scripts or reference files in supporting directories
  5. Test Locally: Verify the skill works with Claude Code before publishing
  6. Publish to GitHub: Push your skill repository for sharing

Example Custom Skill

Create a skill for your specific workflow, like automated testing:

Testing Your Skill

Before publishing, test your skill with Claude Code:

Skills in Cowork

Cowork, the desktop application for AI-assisted document and spreadsheet editing, automatically loads compatible skills to enhance productivity.

Built-in Skill Bundles

Cowork includes specialized skill collections:

  • PDF Skills: Extract, annotate, and transform PDF documents
  • Excel Skills: Analyze data, create pivot tables, build formulas
  • PowerPoint Skills: Design presentations, create visual content
  • Document Skills: Edit, format, and structure documents

Activating Skills in Cowork

Skills are automatically discovered and loaded when you open a document type that matches the skill's triggers. You can also manually enable/disable skills in Cowork's settings panel.

ℹ️
Cowork syncs skills from your global `~/.claude/skills/` directory and project-specific `.claude/skills/` folders. Skills are available across all documents in Cowork.

Popular Skills to Get Started

Explore these widely-adopted skills to enhance your Claude Code workflows:

Development Skills

  • Vercel React Best Practices (vercel/skills@react-best-practices): Comprehensive guidance on React patterns and performance
  • Git Automation (composio/skills@git-automation): Advanced git workflows and branch management
  • Testing Framework (composio/skills@testing-framework): Setup and optimize testing suites

Enterprise Skills

  • API Integration (composio/skills@api-integration): Connect to REST and GraphQL APIs
  • Code Review (composio/skills@code-review): Automated code quality analysis
  • Documentation Generator (composio/skills@docs-generator): Create comprehensive project documentation

Creative Skills

  • Design System (composio/skills@design-system): Build scalable design systems
  • Content Creation (composio/skills@content-creation): Generate marketing and technical content
  • Branding Guidelines (composio/skills@branding): Maintain consistent brand identity

You can browse the complete skills marketplace at agentskills.io or search directly from the CLI.

Publishing Your Skill

Preparing Your Repository

  1. Create a GitHub repository for your skill

  2. Structure your repository:

    my-skill/
├── SKILL.md
├── scripts/
├── references/
├── assets/
└── README.md

  3. Write a comprehensive README explaining the skill's purpose and usage

  4. Tag your repository with agent-skill and skills labels

Registering with skills.sh

  1. Push your skill repository to GitHub

  2. Visit the skills.sh registry and submit your skill

  3. Provide:

    • Repository URL
    • Skill name and description
    • Category and keywords
    • License type

  4. The community reviews and verifies your skill

  5. Once approved, it appears in marketplace searches

Documentation Best Practices

  • Include usage examples in your README
  • Provide clear descriptions of what the skill does
  • Document any external dependencies
  • Add troubleshooting sections for common issues
  • Maintain a CHANGELOG documenting updates

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Skill Not Found in Search

Problem: npx skills find [query] returns no results

Solutions:

  • Try broader search terms (e.g., "react" instead of "react-hooks")
  • Check the skill name on GitHub and search by author
  • Verify the marketplace is accessible: npx skills check-connection

Installation Fails with Permission Error

Problem: Installation fails with permission denied

Solutions:

Skill Not Being Used by Claude

Problem: Installed skill isn't helping with relevant tasks

Solutions:

  • Verify installation: npx skills list
  • Check skill triggers: npx skills info owner/repo@skill-name
  • Review the SKILL.md instructions for clarity
  • Test with explicit mentions: "Use my custom-skill for this task"

Conflicting Skills

Problem: Multiple skills causing unexpected behavior

Solutions:

  • Remove conflicting skills: npx skills remove conflicting-skill
  • Use project-specific installation to isolate skills
  • Document skill compatibility in your project README

Update Failures

Problem: npx skills update fails or rolls back

Solutions:

Best Practices for Skill Management

Organization

  • Use global installation for skills you use across many projects
  • Use project installation for domain-specific skills
  • Document your skill setup in project README files
  • Version control your .claude/skills directory when using project-specific skills

Performance

  • Remove unused skills to reduce context overhead
  • Regularly update to get bug fixes and improvements
  • Monitor skill performance and check usage frequency
  • Consolidate overlapping skills when possible

Security

  • Review skill sources before installation
  • Check license compatibility with your projects
  • Audit installed skills periodically for vulnerabilities
  • Keep skills updated for the latest security patches

Advanced: Skill Configuration

Project-Level Configuration

Create a .claude/skills.config.json to customize skill behavior:

Environment Variables

Set skill configuration via environment variables:

Resources and Community

  • Official Documentation: agentskills.io
  • Skills Registry: Browse and search the complete marketplace
  • Community Forum: Share skills and get feedback from other developers
  • GitHub Discussions: Ask questions and contribute to skill development
  • CLI Help: npx skills --help for all available commands

Conclusion

The skills.sh marketplace empowers you to extend Claude's capabilities with specialized, domain-specific expertise. Whether you're building development workflows, enhancing document editing, or automating business processes, skills provide a powerful way to customize Claude Code and Cowork for your specific needs.

Start by exploring the marketplace, installing skills that match your workflow, and consider contributing your own skills to help other developers build better AI-powered applications.

Last updated: March 9, 2026

Version: 1.0

Sources

  • Agent Skills - Claude Code
  • GitHub - vercel-labs/skills: The open agent skills tool
  • Agent Skills Specification - agentskills.io
  • Skills - npm Package Registry
  • Agent Skills Marketplace - SkillsMP

en.title: Suno AI × Claude Guide — Building an AI-Powered Songwriting Workflow
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to combine Suno AI and Claude for an AI music creation workflow. Covers lyrics generation, MCP integration, and prompt design tips.
en.content:
What Is Suno AI?

Suno AI is an AI music platform that generates full songs from text prompts. Since its launch in 2023, the platform has grown rapidly, reaching 2 million paid subscribers and $300 million in annual recurring revenue as of February 2026.

The latest version, V5, outputs high-quality 44.1 kHz stereo audio with expressive vocals and natural-sounding pronunciation across multiple languages, including Japanese. Songs up to 4 minutes or longer can now be generated in a single pass.

Why Combine Claude with Suno AI?

Suno's music generation is built on three text inputs: lyrics, style tags, and a title. This makes it a natural fit for Claude's text generation strengths.

Claude excels at generating lyrics that match a given theme and mood. It can also help design Suno's style tags (genre, BPM, instrumentation) to achieve the exact atmosphere you're aiming for. And with MCP server integration, you can call the Suno API directly from Claude Code or Cowork, creating a seamless workflow from lyrics to finished track without leaving your conversation.

Basic Workflow: Write Lyrics in Claude, Create Music in Suno

Step 1: Describe Your Theme to Claude

Start by telling Claude about your song's theme, target audience, and mood.

Write lyrics for a nostalgic summer pop song.
The chorus should use short, catchy phrases that are easy to sing along with.
Structure: Verse 1 → Chorus → Verse 2 → Chorus → Bridge → Final Chorus

Claude understands song structure and will output well-organized lyrics with proper sections.

Step 2: Format for Suno

Suno's Custom Mode accepts lyrics with section tags. Ask Claude to format the output:

Format these lyrics for Suno AI's Custom Mode.
Add section tags like [Verse], [Chorus], and [Bridge].

Example output:

[Verse 1]
Fading sunlight on the shore, fireworks echoing far
Footprints in the sand, traces of who we are

[Chorus]
One more time, just one more time
Take me back to that summer night

[Verse 2]
Wind chimes ringing soft and low, memories start to sway
The sweetness on my tongue from a long-gone August day

[Chorus]
One more time, just one more time
Take me back to that summer night

[Bridge]
I always knew that time won't wait
But the smiles in photographs still feel too bright

[Chorus]
One more time, just one more time
Take me back to that summer night

Step 3: Design Style Tags

Suno's style tags control the genre and feel of the generated music. Ask Claude for recommendations:

Suggest Suno AI style tags for these lyrics.
Genre: Pop. Tempo: mid-tempo. Mood: bittersweet and nostalgic.

Claude's suggestion:

Pop, Nostalgic, Mid-tempo, Acoustic Guitar, Female Vocal, Emotional, Summer

Step 4: Generate the Song in Suno

Open Suno's Custom Mode and enter:

  1. Lyrics: The formatted lyrics from Claude (with section tags)
  2. Style of Music: The style tags Claude suggested
  3. Title: Your song title

Click "Create" and two variations will be generated in about 30 seconds to one minute.

Advanced Techniques

Save Your Style in Claude Projects

Claude.ai's Projects feature lets you store your preferred genre and style guidelines as "project knowledge." For example:

Once saved, Claude will consistently generate lyrics and tags matching your style without requiring detailed instructions each time.

Generate Multiple Variations

To explore different directions from the same theme, ask Claude:

Rewrite these lyrics in 3 styles:
1. Upbeat pop (bouncy rhythm for the chorus)
2. Ballad (emphasize the emotional weight)
3. Rock (powerful, punchy words)
Include Suno style tags for each version.

Claude will output three complete lyric sets with matching style tags, so you can generate all three in Suno and compare.

Combine with Suno's Extend Feature

Suno's Extend feature lets you continue a generated song from where it ends. If you need additional lyrics for the extension, ask Claude:

Write a continuation (Verse 3 and Final Chorus) for these lyrics.
Maintain the same tone and theme as the original.
[Paste existing lyrics here]

MCP Server Integration

Overview

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) lets you call the Suno API directly from Claude Code or Cowork, enabling music generation within your conversation flow.

Several community-built Suno MCP servers are available:

  • suno-mcp (GitHub: sandraschi/suno-mcp) — Python-based MCP server
  • suno-mcp (GitHub: CodeKeanu/suno-mcp) — MCP server for Claude Code
  • AceDataCloud MCP Suno — Integrated server via AceDataCloud API

Setup Example (Claude Code)

Add the following to your project's .mcp.json file:

Note that Suno does not offer an official public API, so you'll need to obtain an API key from a third-party provider such as sunoapi.org.

Generating Music via MCP

Once configured, you can create music using natural language directly in Claude Code:

Create a city pop song about driving at night.
Lyrics in Japanese, male vocals, BPM around 120.

Claude will automatically call the Suno API through the MCP server and return the generated track URL in your chat, ready for playback.

Prompt Design Tips

Style Tags by Genre

Here are common style tag combinations to reference when working with Claude:

J-Pop / City Pop: J-Pop, City Pop, Synth, Groovy, Catchy, Japanese

Rock / Alternative: Rock, Alternative, Electric Guitar, Distortion, Energetic, Drums

Electronic / EDM: Electronic, EDM, Synth, Bass Drop, High Energy, Dance

Acoustic / Ballad: Acoustic, Ballad, Piano, Emotional, Slow, Heartfelt

Lo-fi / Chill: Lo-fi, Chill, Hip Hop Beat, Mellow, Relaxing, Ambient

Lyrics Prompt Template

Use this template with Claude for efficient lyrics generation:

Write song lyrics with the following specifications:

Theme: [your theme]
Genre: [genre]
Mood: [upbeat / bittersweet / powerful / calm]
Structure: [Verse-Chorus-Verse-Chorus-Bridge-Chorus]
Language: [English / Japanese / bilingual]
Special instructions: [catchy hook in chorus, rhyming scheme, etc.]

Output format:
- Include Suno section tags
- Include suggested style tags

Pricing and Considerations

Suno AI Plans (as of March 2026)

Basic Plan (Free) provides 50 credits per day. Commercial use of generated songs is not permitted.

Pro Plan ($10/month) provides 2,500 credits per month with commercial use rights.

Premier Plan ($30/month) provides 10,000 credits per month with full access to Suno Studio, the AI-native digital audio workstation.

Copyright Considerations

Following Suno's partnership with Warner Music Group (WMG) in November 2025, the platform's copyright policies have been formalized. Songs created on paid plans can be monetized through streaming and download sales.

However, there is always a possibility that AI-generated music may resemble existing songs. When using generated tracks commercially, review them carefully for any similarities to existing works.

Summary

Combining Suno AI with Claude creates an efficient workflow from ideation to lyrics to finished tracks.

The recommended starting point is simple: use Claude to generate lyrics and design style tags, then paste them into Suno's Custom Mode to generate songs. For a more automated approach, set up an MCP server to call Suno directly from Claude Code or Cowork.

Even without any music production experience, you can create professional-quality songs by simply describing your vision to Claude. Start with Suno's free plan and experiment.

Related Articles

  • Aqua Voice × Claude Guide — Boost your Claude workflow with voice input
  • Cowork Skills & Plugins — Learn the basics of MCP servers
  • Cowork Connectors & MCP Guide — Connecting to external services
  • Claude Code MCP Servers — Deep dive into MCP server setup

en.title: Using Google Stitch as a Complementary Editor — Claude Integration Guide
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: How to use Google Stitch (formerly Antigravity) alongside Claude Code and Cowork. The best way to combine AI coding tools for maximum productivity.
en.content:
What is Google Stitch?

Google Stitch is an AI-powered UI design and web development tool launched by Google Labs in May 2025 at Google I/O. Previously known as Project Antigravity and Project Mariner, this tool is now publicly available as a free experimental project.

Stitch generates complete, production-ready frontend code and visual UI designs from natural language prompts or uploaded images. It leverages the multimodal capabilities of Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro AI model to create a seamless workflow from design ideation to development implementation.

ℹ️
Stitch is completely free to use as part of Google Labs and remains accessible without additional charges as of 2026. Access it at [stitch.withgoogle.com](https://stitch.withgoogle.com/).

Key Features of Google Stitch

1. UI Generation from Text Prompts

Simply describe your application in natural language, and Stitch generates a visually polished UI. By including details about color palettes, layouts, and user experience in your prompt, you achieve more precise results.

2. Rapid Prototyping from Image Inputs

Upload sketches, screenshots of existing apps, or rough wireframes, and Stitch recognizes and converts them into fully digital UIs. Your hand-drawn ideas become functional prototypes in minutes.

3. Production-Ready Code Generation

Stitch outputs aren't just visual designs—they include clean, functional frontend code (HTML/CSS/React). Basic interactive elements are generated automatically, allowing you to immediately proceed with development.

4. Seamless Figma Export

Generated designs can be directly pasted into Figma as Auto Layout components with editable layers. This streamlines collaboration with design teams and integration into existing design systems.

5. Google Cloud & Firebase Integration

Stitch-generated projects are structured to integrate easily with Google Cloud and Firebase, making backend connections and database integrations straightforward from the start.

Claude Code vs Google Stitch: Choosing the Right Tool

Google Stitch and Claude Code are complementary tools with distinct strengths. Using them together creates a more efficient development process.

| Dimension | Google Stitch | Claude Code | |-----------|---------------|-------------| | Core Strength | Visual design, rapid prototyping, automated UI generation | Logic development, testing, refactoring, large-scale projects | | Speed | Extremely fast (complete UI in minutes) | Prioritizes quality and thorough understanding | | Code Quality | Basic UI code generation; optimization often needed | High-quality, maintainable production code | | Complex Logic | Limited (primarily UI interactions) | Extensive support | | Design Patterns | Template-based, standard approaches | Flexible, requirement-driven patterns | | Testing & Debugging | Basic UI testing only | Unit tests, end-to-end tests, comprehensive debugging |

💡
Stitch excels at **design-first thinking**, while Claude is built for **problem-solving-first approaches**. Combining them results in applications that look great and work even better.

Workflow Example 1: Stitch for Visual Design → Claude Code for Logic Integration

This is the design-first approach, ideal for projects where UI is the primary concern.

Step 1: Create UI Prototype with Stitch

  1. Describe your app concept in natural language
  2. Specify details: "A colorful task management app with dark mode support and real-time sync capabilities"
  3. Receive multiple design options within minutes
  4. Select your preferred design and export the HTML/CSS/React code

Step 2: Enhance Features with Claude Code

  1. Upload the Stitch-generated code to Claude Code
  2. Execute requests such as:
    • Add backend API integration logic
    • Implement state management (Redux, Zustand, etc.)
    • Create error handling and input validation
    • Write comprehensive unit tests
    • Optimize performance

Step 3: Return to Stitch for Fine-Tuning if Needed

After feature additions in Claude Code, if UI adjustments are necessary, return to Stitch for visual refinements. Integrate the newly generated code back into Claude Code.

Projects suited to this workflow:

  • MVP (Minimum Viable Product) rapid launches
  • Startup prototypes
  • B2C applications (visually-driven)
  • Design-centric products

Workflow Example 2: Claude Code Foundation → Stitch for Visual Polish

This is the logic-first approach, better suited for existing projects and complex systems.

Step 1: Build Backend & Logic with Claude Code

  1. Design database schemas
  2. Implement API endpoints
  3. Build authentication & authorization logic
  4. Develop business logic
  5. Create comprehensive test suites

Step 2: Generate Frontend with Stitch

  1. Compose a Stitch prompt based on API specifications defined in Claude Code
  2. Describe endpoint details, required forms, and display data
  3. Stitch generates appropriate React components
  4. Minimal adjustments needed to align generated code with Claude Code backend

Step 3: Integration Testing and UI Refinement

Run integration tests in Claude Code to verify frontend-backend coordination. For detailed UI improvements, use Stitch again as needed.

Projects suited to this workflow:

  • Enterprise applications (complex logic)
  • Microservices architectures
  • API-driven development
  • Adding frontends to existing backends

MCP Integration Possibilities

Claude's MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers enable more integrated workflows with Stitch projects in the future.

Anticipated capabilities:

  • Automatically import Stitch-generated projects into Claude Code
  • Call Stitch generation features directly from within Claude Code
  • Synchronize project files automatically
  • Unified change history management across both platforms
ℹ️
MCP integration is on the roadmap with anticipated implementation in 2026. Progress updates will be shared through Claude Lab's official channels.

Comparison with Other AI Coding Tools

Google Stitch occupies a unique position in the AI development tools market.

| Tool | Stitch | Cursor | Replit | Bolt.new | |------|--------|--------|--------|----------| | Primary Focus | Visual UI generation, design-driven development | Code editor with AI assistance | Full-stack IDE | Complete app generation | | Strength Area | Frontend, prototyping, UI implementation | Code editing and completion | Small to mid-size projects | Full-stack app creation | | Learning Curve | Very gentle | Moderate | Low to moderate | Very gentle | | Production Readiness | Initial code generation | High quality | Moderate | Moderate | | Team Collaboration | △ (designer-focused) | ○ | ○ | △ | | Pricing | Free (Google Labs) | Paid | Free to paid | Free to paid |

Stitch excels as a bridge between designers and developers, particularly in UI generation speed and quality.

Best Practices for Effective Collaboration

1. Choose the Right Tool for Your Task

| Question | Answer | Use | |----------|--------|-----| | Need rapid visual prototyping? | Yes | Stitch | | Require complex business logic? | Yes | Claude Code | | Focusing on UI refinement? | Yes | Stitch | | Improving existing code? | Yes | Claude Code |

2. Detailed Prompts Yield Better Results

For Stitch:

❌ Poor: "Make a task management app"

✅ Good: "Kanban-style task board with colorful cards,
dark mode support, real-time sync. Navy header, gradient blue buttons.
Drag-and-drop between columns."

For Claude Code:

❌ Poor: "Add login functionality"

✅ Good: "Implement OAuth 2.0 authentication with JWT tokens.
Email verification on signup. 30-minute session timeout.
Secure logout for lost devices."

3. Version Control and Iteration

  • Always maintain Stitch-generated code in Git
  • Save each version after Claude Code customizations
  • Clearly document each stage when cycling through Stitch-Claude iterations
  • Create meaningful commit messages for audit trails

4. Team Collaboration Structure

Designer → Create UI prototype in Stitch
         ↓ (Export to Figma)
      Team review & approval
         ↓
Developer → Implement features in Claude Code
         ↓ (Deploy)
QA Tester → Integration testing
         ↓ (If needed)
Designer → Fine-tune visuals in Stitch

5. Quality Assurance on Generated Code

While Stitch output is generally solid, verify:

  • Accessibility: Are ARIA labels properly applied?
  • Performance: Any unnecessary re-renders?
  • Security: Potential XSS vulnerabilities?
  • Responsive Design: Mobile and tablet compatible?

Use Claude Code to systematically review and improve these aspects.

6. Apply Domain-Specific Knowledge

  • Simple CRUD apps: Stitch → Claude Code → Complete
  • Regulated industry apps: Claude Code → Stitch (if needed)
  • Multi-tenant SaaS: Claude Code primary, Stitch for prototyping only
  • Gaming/3D graphics: Stitch unsuitable; Claude Code recommended

Important Limitations of Google Stitch

⚠️
Google Stitch specializes in **visual generation** and has known limitations in: - Complex state management logic - Backend API integration (nascent stage) - Advanced security requirements - Performance optimization details - Legacy code compatibility

Always supplement these areas with Claude Code enhancements.

Key Differences in How They Interpret Your Needs

Google Stitch is inherently visual-biased—it thinks in colors, layouts, and components. Claude Code is logic-biased—it thinks in data flows, error handling, and architectural patterns. Understanding this distinction helps you phrase requests more effectively:

| Need | Phrase for Stitch | Phrase for Claude | |------|-------------------|-------------------| | Show user data | "Display customer profiles in card format with avatar images" | "Implement user profile data serialization with caching strategy" | | Handle errors | "Show red notification when action fails" | "Implement exponential backoff retry with error logging" | | Improve speed | "Make loading faster with spinners" | "Implement pagination, lazy loading, and memoization" |

The Synergy: Real-World Development Scenario

Let's trace a realistic project through both tools:

Project: Build a real-time collaboration whiteboard app

Week 1 - Design Phase (Stitch)

  • Prompt: "Design a whiteboard app interface with toolbar, canvas area, color picker, shape tools"
  • Output: Beautiful React UI with proper component structure
  • Export to Figma for team feedback

Week 2 - Logic Development (Claude Code)

  • Import Stitch-generated components
  • Add WebSocket connectivity for real-time collaboration
  • Implement undo/redo functionality
  • Create shape manipulation logic
  • Write comprehensive tests

Week 3 - Refinement (Stitch + Claude)

  • Testers identify UI inconsistencies
  • Return to Stitch to refine specific components
  • Claude Code integrates refinements and optimizes rendering
  • Deploy to production

Result: A production-ready app combining Stitch's design excellence with Claude's engineering rigor—achieved in 3 weeks instead of the 8-10 weeks traditional development would require.

Summary: Building the Future Together

Google Stitch and Claude Code represent the future of software development—specialized tools working in harmony:

  • Stitch excels at speed and visual quality — prototypes, UI generation, design implementation
  • Claude Code excels at quality and complexity — logic, testing, scalability

Combined, they create a powerful, flexible development pipeline for everything from scrappy startups to enterprise systems.

2026 marks the era of AI tooling specialization. Rather than one-size-fits-all solutions, developers now have access to best-in-class tools for specific tasks. Success comes from understanding each tool's strengths and crafting the right combination for your specific project needs.

References

  • Google Developers Blog - Stitch
  • Stitch - Design with AI - Google
  • TechCrunch - Google Launches Stitch
  • GitHub - google-labs-code/stitch-skills
  • Codecademy - Google Stitch Tutorial

en.title: Claude API Batch Processing Guide — Handle Large-Scale Requests Efficiently
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use the Claude API Message Batches feature to process large volumes of requests efficiently and cost-effectively. Ideal for data analysis, content generation, and classification tasks.
en.content:
What Is Batch Processing?

The Claude API's Message Batches feature lets you submit large numbers of requests in bulk for asynchronous processing. Compared to standard API calls, it offers up to 50% cost savings with results delivered within 24 hours.

Use Cases

Data Analysis

Sentiment analysis, text classification, and summarization across thousands of customer reviews.

Content Generation

Bulk generation of product descriptions, metadata, and translated content.

Data Transformation

Batch conversion of unstructured text into JSON or spreadsheet formats.

Basic Usage

Creating a Batch

Example using the Python SDK:

Checking Batch Status

Retrieving Results

Design Considerations

Using custom_id Effectively

Assign a unique custom_id to each request to easily map results back to your original data. Use file names, database IDs, or row numbers.

Error Handling

Individual requests within a batch may fail. Always check the success or failure status of each request when processing results.

Optimizing Batch Size

Each batch can contain up to 10,000 requests. Split larger datasets across multiple batches.

Cost Optimization Tips

  1. Choose the right model: Use Haiku for simple classification tasks
  2. Set max_tokens appropriately: Keep it as low as needed to reduce costs
  3. Always use batches: When real-time responses are not required
  4. Optimize prompts: Write concise prompts to minimize input token usage

Summary

Message Batches is essential for large-scale data processing. With better cost efficiency and relaxed rate limits, it is ideal for periodic data analysis and content generation pipelines.

en.title: Claude Artifacts Guide — Generate Code, Documents, and Diagrams in Real-Time
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude's Artifacts feature to generate and preview code, SVG graphics, React components, Mermaid diagrams, and more within your conversation.
en.content:
What Are Artifacts?

Artifacts is a feature available on Claude.ai that lets you generate content and preview it in real-time during your conversation. Code execution results, React components, SVG graphics, and Mermaid diagrams appear in a panel alongside the chat window.

Supported Content Types

Code Snippets

Write code in Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, and more, displayed with syntax highlighting and a convenient copy button.

React Components

Render interactive UI components on the spot. Tailwind CSS utility classes are available, along with React Hooks like useState and useEffect.

"Create a counter app with dark mode toggle as an Artifact"

SVG Graphics

Preview vector images directly. Great for logo design, icons, and simple illustrations.

Mermaid Diagrams

Generate flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ER diagrams, and Gantt charts using Mermaid syntax, rendered visually.

"Create a user registration flowchart in Mermaid"

HTML Pages

Preview complete HTML pages with CSS and JavaScript. Perfect for landing page prototypes and interactive demos.

Markdown Documents

Display structured documents with formatting. Useful for drafting reports, guides, and documentation.

Practical Use Cases

Prototyping

Quickly visualize ideas by describing what you want. Tell Claude "what about a UI like this" and get a working React component.

Data Visualization

Pass CSV data and ask for "a bar chart" to get an interactive Recharts visualization as an Artifact.

Technical Documentation

Create architecture and sequence diagrams in Mermaid to embed in your technical docs.

Learning and Education

Visualize algorithm behavior through animations or interactively explore mathematical concepts.

Tips for Best Results

  1. Be specific: "Create a dashboard with a line chart for sales data and a pie chart for goal achievement" works better than "make a dashboard"
  2. Iterate gradually: Review the first version, then refine with instructions like "use more muted colors" or "add tooltips to the chart"
  3. Specify libraries: Mention Recharts, D3, Three.js, or other libraries you want, and Claude will generate code using them

Available Libraries

The following libraries can be imported in Artifacts:

  • React — Components and Hooks
  • Recharts — Charts and graphs
  • Lucide React — Icon set
  • D3.js — Data visualization
  • Three.js — 3D graphics
  • Tailwind CSS — Utility-first CSS
  • shadcn/ui — UI components

Summary

Artifacts transforms Claude from a text-based chat into an interactive creative tool. Try it for prototyping, data visualization, documentation, and more.

en.title: Claude Code Git Integration — Automate Commits, PRs, and Reviews
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to leverage Claude Code's Git integration for automatic commit messages, Pull Request creation, and code reviews to streamline your development workflow.
en.content:
Claude Code and Git

Claude Code is a terminal-based AI coding agent with particularly powerful Git and GitHub integration. You can perform routine Git operations simply by giving natural language instructions.

Automatic Commit Messages

Claude Code analyzes your changes and generates appropriate commit messages.

This single command has Claude Code analyze staged changes and propose a Conventional Commits formatted message. The generated messages focus on the "why" rather than the "what" of your changes.

Pull Request Creation

Create PRs using natural language instructions.

"Create a PR for these changes. Explain that we refactored the auth middleware."

Claude Code uses gh pr create, analyzes the changes, and generates an appropriate title and summary. It also automatically suggests a test plan.

Code Review

You can also review GitHub PRs.

"Review PR #42 and check for any security issues."

Claude Code reads the PR diff and provides feedback on code quality, security risks, and performance concerns.

Branch Operations

Create and switch branches with natural language.

"Create and switch to a branch called feature/user-auth."

"Pull the latest from main and resolve any conflicts."

Claude Code also assists with merge conflict resolution by analyzing the conflict and suggesting optimal solutions.

Git Safety Features

Claude Code has built-in safety mechanisms for Git operations.

  • Force push prevention: Won't execute --force pushes unless explicitly authorized
  • Destructive operation warnings: Operations like reset --hard and clean -f require confirmation
  • Sensitive file protection: Prevents committing .env files and credentials
  • Commit preservation: Won't amend existing commits unless explicitly instructed

Practical Workflows

1. Morning Code Review

"List PRs from yesterday and tell me which ones need review."

2. Post-Implementation Commit

"Stage the changes and create a commit message based on what was changed."

3. Release Preparation

"Check the diff against main and draft release notes."

Summary

Claude Code's Git integration significantly streamlines your daily development workflow. Even just automatic commit message generation saves time, but when combined with PR creation and code review, it boosts the entire team's productivity.

en.title: Claude Model Comparison — Choosing Between Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: A detailed comparison of Claude Opus 4, Sonnet 4, and Haiku 3.5 covering performance, speed, and cost. Learn how to choose the right model for your use case.
en.content:
Claude Model Lineup

Anthropic offers three model families designed for different use cases, budgets, and performance requirements. Each has distinct strengths, and the optimal choice depends on the complexity of your tasks, speed needs, and cost constraints.

Claude Opus 4 — Flagship Model

Opus is the top-tier model in the Claude family.

Best For

  • Complex reasoning and multi-step analysis
  • High-quality code generation and review
  • Academic-level long-form writing
  • Nuanced translation work

Characteristics

Opus has longer response times compared to other models, but excels in accuracy and deep insight for challenging problems. It achieves industry-leading scores on coding benchmarks.

Claude Sonnet 4 — Balanced Model

Sonnet delivers the optimal balance of performance and cost.

Best For

  • Everyday chat and Q&A
  • Business document creation and editing
  • Code generation and simple debugging
  • Data analysis and report writing

Characteristics

With performance approaching Opus levels but significantly faster response times, Sonnet offers the best cost-performance ratio for most use cases. It serves as the default model on Claude.ai.

Claude Haiku 3.5 — Fast and Cost-Effective

Haiku is optimized for speed and cost efficiency.

Best For

  • Real-time chatbots
  • High-volume text classification and labeling
  • Simple summarization and format conversion
  • Large-scale batch processing via API

Characteristics

Extremely fast response times with the lowest API costs. For simpler tasks, Haiku delivers quality comparable to Sonnet.

Model Selection Guide

A quick decision framework based on your needs:

  1. Highest quality required (research papers, critical code reviews) → Opus
  2. Balance of quality and speed (daily work, general coding) → Sonnet
  3. Speed and cost are priorities (chatbots, bulk processing) → Haiku

API Pricing

API pricing varies significantly between models, with separate charges for input and output tokens.

  • Opus: Highest per-token cost, but may be more cost-effective overall when it solves problems in fewer interactions
  • Sonnet: Mid-range pricing, recommended for most projects
  • Haiku: Most affordable, ideal for high-volume processing and prototyping

Check the latest pricing on Anthropic's official site.

Summary

If you are unsure, start with Sonnet. Scale up to Opus for quality or down to Haiku for cost—a gradual approach that optimizes your workflow.

en.title: CLAUDE.md and Auto Memory — Teaching Claude Code Your Project
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use CLAUDE.md files to communicate coding standards and project information to Claude Code, plus how Auto Memory automatically accumulates learnings.
en.content:
What Is CLAUDE.md?

CLAUDE.md is a markdown file that Claude Code automatically reads at the start of every session. Place it in your project root to communicate coding standards, architecture decisions, preferred libraries, and review checklists to Claude.

Why You Need It

Claude Code starts fresh each session without context from previous interactions. With CLAUDE.md, you never need to repeat the same explanations, and project-specific rules are applied consistently.

Writing CLAUDE.md

Create a CLAUDE.md file in your project root.

File Locations and Priority

CLAUDE.md files can be placed in multiple locations with different scopes:

  1. Project root (./CLAUDE.md): Applies to the entire project
  2. Subdirectories (./src/CLAUDE.md): Applies to specific directories
  3. Home directory (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md): Applies to all projects

When multiple files exist, all are loaded, with more specific scopes taking priority.

Auto Memory

Claude Code includes an Auto Memory feature that automatically saves learnings during work.

What Gets Remembered

  • Project build commands and their results
  • Problems discovered during debugging and their solutions
  • Frequently used file paths and patterns
  • User preferences and workflows

Benefits

With Auto Memory, Claude Code deepens its project understanding the more you use it—without manually updating CLAUDE.md. Learnings from previous sessions carry forward.

Team Usage

Commit CLAUDE.md to your repository so all team members share the same instructions with Claude Code.

# Review Checklist
Before creating a PR, verify:
- [ ] No TypeScript type errors (tsc --noEmit)
- [ ] All tests pass (npm test)
- [ ] No ESLint warnings (npm run lint)
- [ ] OpenAPI spec updated if API changes were made

Tips for Writing Effective CLAUDE.md

  1. Keep it concise: Claude reads it every session, so remove unnecessary info
  2. Use structure: Organize with headers and lists
  3. Keep it updated: Evolve CLAUDE.md as your project changes
  4. Be specific: Instead of "write good code," say "keep functions under 30 lines"

Summary

CLAUDE.md significantly impacts Claude Code's performance. Spending five minutes writing one improves every interaction and boosts team productivity. Combined with Auto Memory, Claude Code gets smarter the more you use it.

en.title: Cowork Browser Automation Guide — Let Claude Handle Web Tasks
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Cowork's browser automation feature (Claude in Chrome) to delegate website navigation, form filling, data collection, and more to Claude.
en.content:
What Is Cowork Browser Automation?

Cowork's Claude in Chrome feature lets you delegate browser operations to Claude. You can automate web page browsing, form filling, information gathering, screenshot capture, and more using natural language instructions.

Basic Usage

Page Browsing

Have Claude open websites and extract content for you.

"Open the Anthropic website and tell me the titles of the latest blog posts"

Claude operates browser tabs to access pages, analyzes content, and returns the information you need.

Form Filling

Automate web form input. Note that sensitive data like passwords and banking information is restricted for security.

"Fill in my name and address on this form"

Screenshots

Capture the current state of a page for visual verification.

"Take a screenshot of this page"

Use Cases

Information Gathering

Ideal for collecting and comparing information across multiple websites.

"Check the prices for Tokyo to Osaka flights on three travel sites and compare them"

Website Testing

Have Claude verify your website's display and functionality.

"Open my site example.com and check if the mobile layout looks correct"

Data Entry Automation

Automate repetitive data entry tasks.

"Use the data from this spreadsheet to fill in the web form entries one by one"

Security and Limitations

Browser automation comes with several important restrictions.

Protected Operations

  • Password entry: Login credentials must be entered manually
  • Financial data: Credit card numbers and bank details are never handled by Claude
  • Account creation: New accounts must be created manually
  • Permission changes: Document sharing and access control changes require manual action

Operations Requiring Confirmation

Claude will ask for confirmation before performing these actions:

  • File downloads
  • Form submissions (clicking "Submit" or "Post" buttons)
  • Sending emails or messages
  • Accepting terms and conditions
  • Completing purchases or payments

Tips for Effective Use

  1. Be specific: Clearly describe the target buttons, links, or elements
  2. Work in steps: Break complex operations into stages and verify at each step
  3. Verify results: Use screenshots to confirm each step's outcome

Summary

Cowork's browser automation is a powerful tool for streamlining everyday web tasks. Understanding its security limitations will help you leverage it effectively for information gathering and data entry.

en.title: Cowork Scheduled Tasks — Automate Your Recurring Work
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Cowork's scheduled task feature to automate file management, report generation, data backups, and other recurring tasks with Claude.
en.content:
What Are Scheduled Tasks?

Cowork's scheduled tasks let you have Claude automatically execute tasks at specified times or intervals. Automate morning reports, weekly file cleanup, periodic data checks, and more.

What You Can Automate

File Management

  • Regular cleanup of the Downloads folder
  • Archiving old files
  • Batch file renaming

Report and Document Generation

  • Weekly summary reports
  • Graph generation from data files
  • Meeting agenda preparation

Data Monitoring

  • Spreadsheet anomaly checking
  • File change monitoring
  • Regular backups

Setting Up Schedules

Create a scheduled task in Cowork by specifying what to do and how often.

Basic Examples

"Every morning at 9am, move files older than 1 week from Downloads to the Archive folder"

"Every Monday morning, create a weekly report from last week's sales data"

Cron Expression Scheduling

For finer control, use cron expressions:

  • 0 9 * * * — Every day at 9:00 AM
  • 0 9 * * 1-5 — Weekdays at 9:00 AM
  • 30 8 * * 1 — Every Monday at 8:30 AM
  • 0 0 1 * * — First day of every month at midnight

Best Practices

1. Automate Gradually

Start by running tasks manually to verify results, then set up the schedule once you are confident.

2. Write Clear Instructions

Be specific with task descriptions. Instead of "organize files," say "move .pdf files from Downloads to Documents/PDFs."

3. Check Results Regularly

Periodically review automated task results to ensure everything works as expected.

Important Notes

  • Scheduled tasks only run while the Cowork desktop app is active
  • Be careful with tasks that involve deleting important files
  • Run new tasks manually once before scheduling to verify behavior

Summary

Scheduled tasks free you from repetitive daily work. Start small and gradually expand the scope of automation.

en.title: Extended Thinking Explained — Leverage Claude's Reasoning Process
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn about Claude's Extended Thinking feature. Get more accurate and deeper responses for complex reasoning tasks by letting Claude think step by step before answering.
en.content:
What Is Extended Thinking?

Extended Thinking is a feature that allows Claude to show its step-by-step reasoning process before delivering a final answer. By explicitly unfolding the reasoning that would normally happen internally, Claude produces more accurate responses for complex problems.

Why It Matters

When humans solve a difficult math problem, they don't jump straight to the answer—they work through it on paper. Extended Thinking gives Claude the same "think on paper" process.

High-Impact Tasks

  • Multi-step mathematical proofs
  • Complex code debugging and architecture
  • Legal document and contract analysis
  • Scientific hypothesis verification
  • Logical structuring of long-form content

Limited Impact Tasks

  • Simple Q&A ("What is the capital of Japan?")
  • Routine document creation
  • Short translations

Using It on Claude.ai

On Claude.ai, no special configuration is needed. Claude automatically adjusts thinking depth based on question complexity. You can also explicitly request deeper thinking.

"Please think through this problem carefully, step by step, before answering"

Using It via API

Enable Extended Thinking by setting the thinking parameter in your API request.

Parameters

  • type: "enabled" for manual activation. Claude Opus 4.6 also supports "adaptive"
  • budget_tokens: Maximum tokens for thinking. Must be less than max_tokens

Adaptive Thinking

Claude Opus 4.6 introduces Adaptive Thinking, a new mode that automatically adjusts thinking depth.

No budget_tokens needed—Claude automatically calibrates how deeply to reason based on problem complexity.

Cost Considerations

Extended Thinking tokens are billable. Thinking tokens count as output tokens, so your budget_tokens setting directly affects cost.

Setting a large thinking budget for simple questions wastes money. Match the budget to task complexity for optimal cost-quality balance.

Practical Examples

Code Review

thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 8000}

"Check this code for security vulnerabilities, testing against each common attack pattern"

Data Analysis

thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 12000}

"Analyze the trends in this sales data and provide a forecast with supporting evidence"

Summary

Extended Thinking transforms Claude from an "instant answer machine" into a "deep thinking partner." Use it actively for complex reasoning tasks and stick with standard mode for simple ones to optimize the balance between quality and cost.

en.title: MCP Servers Guide — Extend Claude Code's Capabilities
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Model Context Protocol (MCP) to add custom tools to Claude Code. Build MCP servers for database connections, API integrations, file operations, and more.
en.content:
What Is MCP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard protocol that enables AI models to interact with external tools and data sources. Through MCP servers, Claude Code can add capabilities like database queries, API calls, and file system operations.

How MCP Works

MCP is a communication protocol between a client (Claude Code) and servers (tool providers).

  1. Servers publish a list of available tools with descriptions
  2. Claude Code recognizes these tools and calls them as needed
  3. Servers execute the tool and return results
  4. Claude Code interprets results and determines next actions

Configuring MCP Servers

Add MCP servers to your Claude Code configuration file.

Configuration file locations:

  • Per-project: .claude/settings.json
  • Per-user: ~/.claude/settings.json

Key Official MCP Servers

Examples of official servers provided by Anthropic and the community.

File System

Safely read, write, and manage files and directories.

PostgreSQL / SQLite

Connect to databases, execute queries, and retrieve schema information.

GitHub

Manage issues, operate on PRs, and retrieve repository information.

Slack

Send and receive messages and manage channels.

Web Browser

Fetch and scrape web pages.

Building Custom MCP Servers

You can build custom MCP servers in TypeScript.

Security Considerations

MCP servers are powerful but require security awareness.

  • Use trusted servers only: Review third-party MCP server code before deployment
  • Principle of least privilege: Use read-only database accounts when possible
  • Credential management: Store API keys in environment variables, never hardcode in config files

Summary

MCP transforms Claude Code from a coding assistant into a versatile agent that can connect with any system. Start with official servers and build custom ones as your needs grow.

en.title: Prompt Caching Guide — Reduce API Costs by Up to 90%
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Claude API's prompt caching feature to dramatically reduce costs for repeated system prompts and context. A must-know for API developers.
en.content:
What Is Prompt Caching?

Claude API's prompt caching reuses the same context across API requests to significantly reduce input token costs. When you repeatedly send requests with identical system prompts or documents, subsequent requests receive substantial discounts.

When to Use It

Prompt caching is especially effective in these patterns:

  • Including long system prompts in every request
  • Asking multiple questions while referencing a large document
  • Reusing the same few-shot examples
  • Sending the same context frequently in RAG applications

Basic Usage

Specify cache_control on the parts you want to cache.

cache_control Types

  • ephemeral: Cache persists for a minimum of 5 minutes (TTL resets on each use)

Cost Structure

Three pricing tiers apply based on cache state:

  1. Cache write: 25% surcharge on normal input token price (first time only)
  2. Cache read: 90% discount on normal input token price
  3. Normal input: Standard pricing for non-cached portions

This means the same context costs 1/10th from the second request onward.

Checking Cache in Responses

Monitor cache effectiveness via the usage field in responses.

Second request:

Design Best Practices

Place Cacheable Content First

Put static content at the beginning of your message array and variable content at the end.

Ensure Sufficient Length

Cached content must be at least 1,024 tokens (2,048 for Claude Haiku). Shorter content won't create a cache.

Design Cache Breakpoints

You can specify cache_control in up to 4 places. Set them at reusable boundaries: system prompts, documents, few-shot examples.

Practical Example: Chatbot

With this design, tens of thousands of system prompt tokens are processed at 1/10th cost from the second request onward.

Summary

Prompt caching delivers significant cost savings, especially for applications with long contexts. Since it only requires thoughtful system prompt design, every API developer should implement this feature.

en.title: Claude Code CLI Commands — A Complete Guide to Every Command and Flag
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: A comprehensive guide to Claude Code CLI commands and flags. Covers interactive mode, one-shot execution, MCP management, authentication, and configuration.
en.content:
Claude Code CLI Overview

Claude Code centers around the claude command, offering a rich set of subcommands and flags. This guide systematically covers all commands based on the official CLI reference.

Core Commands

Interactive Mode

Running without arguments launches interactive mode. You can have a back-and-forth conversation with Claude while coding.

Quick Queries

Pass a short query directly to start interactive mode with that prompt sent immediately.

One-Shot Mode (-p)

The -p (--print) flag executes a single task non-interactively. Output goes to stdout, making it perfect for piping and redirection.

Key Flags

Model Selection

Use --model to specify which model to use. Default is Claude Sonnet, but Opus delivers higher accuracy for complex tasks.

Directory Addition

--add-dir adds folders outside the working directory to Claude Code's context. Useful for monorepos and shared libraries.

Tool Restrictions

--allowedTools restricts which tools Claude Code can use. Essential for safe execution in CI/CD pipelines.

System Prompt Extension

--append-system-prompt appends custom rules to the system prompt. Great for enforcing coding conventions.

Authentication Commands

ℹ️
The first run automatically starts the browser-based auth flow. For API key usage, set the `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable.

Configuration Commands

MCP (Model Context Protocol) Management

MCP servers let you connect Claude Code to external services like Slack, GitHub, and databases.

Slash Commands (Inside Interactive Mode)

| Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | /help | Show help | | /clear | Clear conversation history | | /compact | Summarize and compress context | | /cost | Show token usage | | /doctor | Diagnose configuration issues | | /init | Create CLAUDE.md project config | | /review | Start a code review | | /terminal-setup | Configure terminal key bindings |

💡
`/compact` is especially useful during long sessions when the context window fills up. It summarizes the conversation so you can keep working without interruption.

Practical Use Cases

Automated Code Review in CI/CD

Batch File Processing

Resume Previous Session

-r (--resume) resumes your last session. Handy when you need to pick up where you left off.

Next Steps

  • CLAUDE.md Configuration — Project-specific settings for more accurate code generation
  • Plugin Development — Extend functionality with MCP servers and skills
  • GitHub Actions Integration — Embed AI into your CI/CD with background tasks

en.title: Claude Code Plugin Development — Build Your First Plugin
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: A guide to the Claude Code plugin system. Learn about Skills, Agents, MCP servers, and Hooks, with practical examples of writing plugin manifests and building your first plugin.
en.content:
Plugin System Overview

Claude Code plugins are packages that extend Claude Code's capabilities. A plugin can include up to five component types:

  • Skills — Prompt templates and workflow definitions
  • Agents — Autonomous agents specialized for specific tasks
  • MCP servers — External service integrations (Model Context Protocol)
  • Hooks — Event-driven automation (e.g., auto-processing on file changes)
  • LSP servers — Language Server Protocol for editor integration

Plugin Structure

Plugins follow this directory layout:

my-plugin/
├── plugin.json        # Manifest file (required)
├── skills/
│   └── my-skill/
│       └── SKILL.md   # Skill definition
├── agents/
│   └── my-agent.md    # Agent definition
├── mcp/
│   └── server.js      # MCP server implementation
└── hooks/
    └── on-save.sh     # Hook handler

The plugin.json Manifest

The core of every plugin is plugin.json:

ℹ️
For the complete manifest field specification, see the official Plugins Reference documentation.

Installation Scopes

Plugins can be installed at four scopes:

| Scope | Description | Use Case | |-------|-------------|----------| | user | User-global | Personal shared tools | | project | Project-specific | Team-shared config | | local | Local only | Experimental plugins | | managed | Organization-managed | Enterprise deployments |

CLI Plugin Management

Tutorial: Build a Simple Skill Plugin

Let's create a code review skill plugin as the simplest example.

1. Create the Directory

2. Create plugin.json

3. Create the Skill File

skills/review/SKILL.md:

4. Install Locally

💡
Use `--scope local` during development, then publish to npm to share with other users.

Integrating MCP Servers

Including an MCP server in your plugin lets Claude Code access external APIs and databases.

⚠️
MCP servers run in your local environment. Only install from trusted sources and manage secrets via environment variables.

Next Steps

  • Hooks — Automate actions on file saves or pre-commit
  • Agent Development — Build autonomous task-specific workflows
  • Publishing Plugins — Share via the npm registry

en.title: Claude Code Setup Guide — From Installation to Your First Run
en.author: Claude Lab
en.description: Learn how to install and use Claude Code. The fastest path to AI-driven development from your terminal.
en.content:
What is Claude Code?

Claude Code is a command-line tool from Anthropic that lets you delegate coding tasks to Claude directly from your terminal. It deeply integrates with your development workflow — reading and writing files, running Git operations, executing tests, and more.

ℹ️
Claude Code requires a Max plan ($100/month) or an API key. It is not available on the Pro plan ($20/month).

Installation

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 18 or later
  • npm or yarn
  • Anthropic account (Max plan or API key)

Install Command

Verify the installation:

Initial Setup

Running claude for the first time starts the authentication flow.

A browser window will open asking you to log in with your Anthropic account. Once authenticated, Claude Code is ready to use in your terminal.

Basic Usage

Interactive Mode (Default)

In interactive mode, you can have a back-and-forth conversation with Claude while working on your project.

One-Shot Mode

The -p flag executes a single task. This is useful for scripts and CI/CD pipelines.

Common Commands

| Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | claude | Start interactive mode | | claude -p "..." | Execute a one-shot task | | claude -r | Resume the last session | | claude config | View/change settings | | claude mcp | Manage MCP servers |

Slash Commands

Inside interactive mode, you can use slash commands:

/help        — Show help
/clear       — Clear conversation history
/compact     — Summarize and compress context
/cost        — Show token usage for current session
/doctor      — Diagnose configuration issues
/init        — Create a CLAUDE.md project config file
/review      — Start a code review
/terminal-setup — Configure terminal key bindings
💡
The `CLAUDE.md` file created by `/init` is where you describe your project's coding conventions and architecture. Claude Code reads it automatically to generate context-aware code.

Configure Your Project with CLAUDE.md

Create a CLAUDE.md file at your project root to give Claude Code context about your project.

MCP (Model Context Protocol)
